proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Validating and Extending Ground Motion Scaling Laws Under Diverse Dynamic Shaking Patterns
proposals.motivation: Current ground motion scaling laws (GSLs) are foundational for seismic hazard analysis and structural design, but their validation often relies on limited suites of earthquake motions, primarily scaled by peak ground acceleration (PGA) or spectral acceleration at a single period. This pre-2026 practice overlooks the influence of complex shaking patterns—such as multi-frequency content, duration, and sequence of strong pulses—on structural damage, potentially leading to unconservative or inefficient designs. A systematic validation under a wider variety of input motions is crucial for improving the reliability of performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalizability of existing GSLs is limited by their calibration to narrow bands of ground motion characteristics; we hypothesize that incorporating explicit representations of dynamic shaking patterns (e.g., via wavelet coefficients or spectral shape parameters) into a physics-informed machine learning model will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of scaled response predictions across a broad spectrum of realistic earthquakes.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid Physics-Informed DeepONet (PI-DeepONet) architecture. The operator network will learn a mapping from input ground motion time histories (encoded via wavelet packet transform for pattern representation) to scaled structural response spectra. Physics will be enforced through custom stiffness- and energy-based loss functions, informed by the researcher's prior work, to ensure predictions adhere to fundamental mechanics. A comprehensive database of recorded motions (e.g., from PEER NGA-West2) will be used for training and testing, with motions categorized by dynamic pattern features (pulse-like, long-duration, high-frequency) to challenge the scaling law.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, extensible framework for assessing the limits of conventional GSLs, directly contributing to more accurate seismic risk assessment. It will provide the community with a novel tool for generating scenario-specific scaling factors that account for dynamic pattern effects, moving beyond simple intensity measures. The integrated PI-DeepONet model will serve as a benchmark for incorporating physics into data-driven seismic analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of dynamic shaking pattern descriptors into the scaling law validation process via a physics-informed operator learning framework, moving beyond scalar intensity measures. This combines the researcher's expertise in physics-informed DeepONets and stiffness-based loss with a systematic pattern-based analysis of ground motion suites, a synthesis not yet explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Facility Validation Framework for Physics-Informed Machine Learning Models in Geotechnical Centrifuge Testing
proposals.motivation: The validation of advanced predictive models for soil-structure interaction, such as Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and DeepONets, is often limited to data from a single centrifuge facility or a narrow set of experimental conditions. This limits confidence in model generalizability for real-world scenarios where soil properties, boundary conditions, and instrumentation effects vary. A systematic framework to test model robustness against the inherent variability across different centrifuge facilities is critically needed to advance the field's trust in data-driven and hybrid simulation tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Physics-informed machine learning models that incorporate facility-specific discrepancies as part of their loss function or architecture will demonstrate significantly improved predictive robustness and generalizability when validated against a multi-facility experimental dataset, compared to models trained on data from a single source.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark dataset from pre-2026 centrifuge tests on similar soil-structure systems (e.g., liquefiable slopes, shallow foundations) conducted at multiple facilities (e.g., RPI, UC Davis, KAIST). Using this dataset, we will train and validate a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, extending recent pre-2026 work by the researcher. A novel, multi-fidelity loss function will be designed, combining a primary physics-informed term (governed by wave propagation equations), a data-fitting term from a 'primary' facility, and a regularization term that penalizes large deviations from trends observed in 'secondary' facility data. Bayesian optimization will be used to tune hyperparameters that balance these loss components.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A novel, open-access multi-facility benchmark dataset for geotechnical model validation, 2) A new 'facility-robust' Physics-Informed DeepONet framework with a structured loss function, and 3) Quantitative metrics for assessing model generalizability across experimental variability, moving validation beyond single-source correlations.
proposals.novelty: While multi-fidelity modeling and physics-informed ML are established, explicitly encoding inter-facility experimental variability as a constitutive component of the model's loss function to *systematically* test and enhance generalizability is a novel and non-obvious application of these pre-2026 techniques to the geotechnical validation challenge.

proposals.title: A Formal Verification and Validation Framework for Liquefaction Numerical Models: Adapting Hierarchical Procedures from Computational Fluid Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Numerical modeling of soil liquefaction is critical for seismic risk assessment and design, yet model validation remains ad-hoc and lacks standardization. This leads to significant uncertainty in model predictions and hinders their confident adoption in engineering practice. A rigorous, structured validation protocol, akin to those established in mature fields like computational fluid dynamics (CFD), is urgently needed to quantify and build confidence in liquefaction simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the formal hierarchical verification and validation (V&V) procedures and metrics from CFD, we can establish a systematic, traceable framework that significantly improves the credibility and predictive accuracy of advanced constitutive and coupled numerical models for soil liquefaction.
proposals.method: We will map the ASME V&V 20-2009 standard for CFD to geotechnical modeling, defining a hierarchy of validation benchmarks from element tests (e.g., cyclic direct simple shear) to centrifuge model tests. For each level, we will quantify numerical error (verification) via grid convergence studies using the Grid Convergence Index. We will then quantify model form error (validation) by comparing high-fidelity simulations (using PM4Sand, SANISAND) against experimental data, employing validation metrics such as the Spalding and Liu metrics for time-history and spatial field comparisons. A key step will be the explicit separation of numerical and model-form uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first standardized V&V protocol for liquefaction modeling, providing a clear roadmap for model developers and users. It will deliver quantitative error estimates for common constitutive models under varied loading conditions, directly informing their appropriate use in practice. The framework will elevate the rigor of geotechnical numerical analysis, fostering greater confidence in model-based decision-making for critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While V&V is mature in CFD, its formal application to geotechnical constitutive and boundary value problem modeling is novel. The explicit adaptation of hierarchical benchmarks and CFD-specific validation metrics to soil liquefaction problems represents a significant methodological advance beyond conventional qualitative or point-based model comparisons.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Multi-Scale Soil Constitutive Model Calibration from Centrifuge to Element Tests
proposals.motivation: Advanced constitutive models for soil (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) are essential for predicting soil-structure interaction under seismic loads in centrifuge tests and numerical simulations. However, their calibration relies heavily on direct simple shear or triaxial element tests, creating a scale gap with system-level centrifuge performance. This disconnect leads to significant uncertainty in model parameters when back-calculated from centrifuge data, hindering predictive accuracy and the transfer of physical insights across scales.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed machine learning operator, trained on paired centrifuge and element test datasets, can learn a robust mapping to infer optimal constitutive model parameters from element test results that accurately reproduce the system-level response observed in centrifuge experiments.
proposals.method: We will develop a Multiple-Input Deep Operator Network (DeepONet) architecture, informed by the researcher's prior work. The branch net will process time-series data from element tests (e.g., stress-strain curves from DSS). The trunk net will encode boundary conditions and loading paths from target centrifuge model configurations. The network will be trained to output the parameters of a target constitutive model. Physics will be embedded through custom stiffness-based loss functions, penalizing deviations from known stress-strain relationships, and a Bayesian optimization loop will be integrated to efficiently explore the parameter space and enhance explainability of the learned mappings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, data-driven pipeline for constitutive model calibration that directly bridges the element-to-centrifuge scale gap. It will yield more reliable and physically consistent model parameters for seismic soil-structure interaction analysis. The framework will also enhance the interpretability of complex soil behavior by revealing which element test features most critically influence system-level performance.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to either element test analysis or centrifuge result prediction in isolation, the novel integration of a Physics-Informed DeepONet as a dedicated 'translator' between these two specific experimental scales for constitutive model calibration is unexplored. The method moves beyond black-box prediction to a calibration tool that respects underlying soil mechanics.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Bayesian Deep Operator Network for Multi-Facility Inverse Calibration of Soil Constitutive Models
proposals.motivation: Calibrating advanced soil constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) for high-fidelity numerical simulations remains a critical bottleneck in geotechnical earthquake engineering. Traditional calibration against single centrifuge tests is often non-unique and fails to generalize. Leveraging the growing ensemble of public centrifuge data from facilities like NEES and the George E. Brown, Jr. Network provides a rich, multi-fidelity dataset to overcome this limitation and develop robust, transferable model parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed, multi-fidelity machine learning framework can uniquely and efficiently invert an ensemble of centrifuge test results to determine a generalized set of soil model parameters that provide accurate predictions across a wide range of boundary conditions and loading scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, extending the researcher's prior work, where the branch net encodes multi-facility experimental data (e.g., acceleration, displacement time histories) and the trunk net queries soil parameters and loading conditions. The loss function will integrate a stiffness-based physics regularizer with a Bayesian optimization layer for uncertainty quantification. The framework will be trained and validated on curated datasets from the NEEShub repository, targeting PM4Sand as the initial constitutive model for calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, automated, and generalizable framework for soil model calibration, directly increasing the reliability of numerical simulations for performance-based design. It will also produce a publicly available, benchmarked set of inversely calibrated parameters for common centrifuge model configurations, serving as a valuable resource for both researchers and practitioners.
proposals.novelty: This integrates the multi-fidelity experimental ensemble concept with a physics-informed DeepONet for geotechnical inverse analysis, moving beyond single-test calibration or purely data-driven approaches. The explicit incorporation of a stiffness-based loss function within a Bayesian inverse operator network is a novel synthesis of the researcher's established methods.

proposals.title: Robustness and Generalization Assessment of Physics-Informed Neural Operators Using a Broad Spectrum of Seismic Motions
proposals.motivation: Current physics-informed machine learning models for structural response prediction are often trained and validated on limited, prescribed ground motions, such as those from specific design codes. This raises concerns about their robustness and generalization to the wide variability of real earthquake records, which differ in frequency content, duration, and intensity. Assessing model performance across a broader suite of motions is crucial for building confidence in these data-driven tools for practical seismic analysis and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that physics-informed neural operators (e.g., DeepONet, MIONet) trained on a diverse, spectrally rich database of ground motions will demonstrate superior generalization, reduced prediction bias, and more reliable uncertainty quantification for structural responses compared to models trained on a narrow set of prescribed motions.
proposals.method: We will curate a large, varied dataset of recorded ground motions from repositories like PEER NGA-West2, categorizing them by source mechanism, distance, and site condition. This dataset will be used to train and test the researcher's existing Physics-informed DeepONet and MIONet frameworks. The training will incorporate stiffness-based and physics-constrained loss functions to ensure physical consistency. Model performance will be rigorously evaluated using error metrics and explainable AI (XAI) techniques, such as SHAP, to analyze how motion characteristics influence prediction accuracy and model attention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for validating the robustness of physics-informed ML models in seismic applications. It will deliver practical guidelines for selecting training motions to ensure reliable extrapolation and will produce benchmarked, open-source model architectures pre-trained on a comprehensive motion suite for the community.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have applied physics-informed ML to seismic problems, the explicit, rigorous testing of these models' generalization capabilities across a broad, real-world spectrum of ground motions—integrated with XAI for model interpretability—remains an underexplored and critical step toward their engineering adoption.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Bayesian Learning Framework for Validating and Calibrating Soil Constitutive Models Under High-Intensity Seismic Shaking (>0.25 g)
proposals.motivation: Current numerical models for soil-structure interaction are often calibrated against moderate shaking records (<0.25 g), but their predictive accuracy for high-intensity motions, where nonlinear and cyclic degradation effects dominate, remains uncertain. Validating these constitutive models for stronger shaking is critical for assessing the performance of critical infrastructure under extreme seismic hazards, a core challenge in performance-based earthquake engineering. This gap limits confidence in numerical predictions for lifeline systems and high-consequence facilities.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hybrid framework integrating physics-informed neural operators with Bayesian optimization can systematically identify and correct biases in advanced soil constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) when subjected to high-intensity ground motions, leading to significantly improved predictive accuracy for key engineering demand parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, similar to the researcher's prior work, to learn the mapping between input ground motions (scaled to >0.25 g) and soil/structural response outputs from high-fidelity finite element models. A stiffness-based loss function will enforce known physical constraints. This operator will be integrated into a Bayesian optimization loop to efficiently calibrate the parameters of target constitutive models by minimizing the discrepancy against a curated database of centrifuge test data (e.g., from NEES) and recorded case histories for high-intensity events. The explainability component from prior work will be used to interpret model discrepancies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, calibrated, and explainable framework for simulating soil-structure systems under high-intensity shaking, directly addressing a key uncertainty in performance-based design. It will produce a publicly available dataset of benchmarked model parameters for common constitutive laws at high strains. The methodology advances the integration of physics-informed machine learning with traditional geotechnical modeling for extreme loading.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a Physics-Informed DeepONet as a surrogate within a Bayesian calibration loop specifically targeted at the high-intensity shaking regime, moving beyond simple curve-fitting to a physically consistent, efficient, and interpretable validation protocol for complex soil models.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Bayesian Optimization Framework for Automated Calibration of Advanced Soil Constitutive Models
proposals.motivation: Accurate constitutive models for soil are critical for reliable geotechnical seismic analysis and design. However, calibrating their numerous parameters from laboratory test data (e.g., cyclic triaxial, resonant column) is a complex, often manual, and subjective process, leading to significant model uncertainty. This bottleneck hinders the high-fidelity, data-informed site response and soil-structure interaction analyses central to modern performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, automated calibration framework, inspired by robust parameter identification methods in metallurgy and materials science, can efficiently and objectively identify optimal soil model parameters, yielding more accurate and reliable predictions of soil nonlinear and hysteretic behavior under dynamic loading.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid physics-informed machine learning calibration pipeline. First, we will employ Bayesian Optimization (BO) with Gaussian Processes, as used in the researcher's prior work for hyperparameter tuning, to intelligently explore the high-dimensional parameter space of advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND). The objective function will minimize the discrepancy between model simulations and lab data across stress-strain loops. To enhance efficiency and enforce physical plausibility, we will integrate a physics-informed neural network (PINN) or a DeepONet architecture, pre-trained on a spectrum of model simulations, to act as a fast surrogate within the BO loop, penalizing non-physical parameter combinations through customized stiffness-based loss functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source computational tool for the geotechnical community to reduce subjectivity in model calibration. It will advance the state-of-the-art in data-driven soil modeling by providing a rigorous, reproducible methodology for parameter identification, directly improving the accuracy of subsequent seismic site response and structural dynamic analyses. The integration of BO with physics-informed surrogates represents a significant step toward trustworthy automation in geotechnical simulation.
proposals.novelty: While BO and PINNs have been applied separately in civil engineering, their integrated use for constitutive model calibration is novel. This approach uniquely adapts systematic optimization paradigms from materials science to the distinct challenges of soil mechanics, moving beyond traditional trial-and-error or gradient-based methods to provide a global, efficient, and physics-constrained calibration solution.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Element-Level Calibration of Soil Constitutive Models Using Discrete Element Simulations
proposals.motivation: Continuum models for soil-structure interaction, crucial for seismic analysis, rely on phenomenological constitutive laws calibrated at the macro-scale, often masking complex particle-scale mechanisms. This scale gap introduces significant uncertainty in predicting localized damage and failure, especially under cyclic loading. Validating and refining these models at the fundamental element level is essential for improving the reliability of geotechnical simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physics-informed machine learning operator, trained on high-fidelity Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations of soil element tests, can directly infer optimal, history-dependent parameters for macroscopic constitutive models, leading to more accurate and physically consistent predictions of soil response under complex loading paths.
proposals.method: We will generate a high-fidelity dataset using 3D DEM simulations of representative soil volumes undergoing element tests (e.g., cyclic triaxial, simple shear). This data will train a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, where the branch net encodes the loading path and the trunk net evaluates constitutive model parameters. The network loss will integrate a physics-based component, penalizing the discrepancy between the DEM-measured stress-strain response and the output of a differentiable, embedded constitutive model (e.g., a SANICLAY-type framework) evaluated with the DeepONet-predicted parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, DEM-informed calibration pipeline that directly bridges particle-scale mechanics to continuum model parameters, moving beyond traditional curve-fitting. It will provide a validated, element-level benchmark for constitutive models used in geotechnical practice. The resulting framework will enhance the predictive accuracy of soil-structure interaction simulations for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: The integration of DEM data with a Physics-Informed DeepONet to act as a direct, differentiable calibrator for complex constitutive models is a novel approach. It uniquely enforces physics consistency within the learning process, unlike purely data-driven surrogates, enabling the discovery of interpretable parameter evolution laws.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Neural Operator Validation for Seismic Site Response in Complex, Layered Soil Deposits Using the LEAP Framework
proposals.motivation: Current validation frameworks for seismic site response models, such as LEAP (Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects), have primarily focused on uniform or simple soil profiles. In practice, natural soil deposits are often layered with varying stiffness and saturation, which significantly influences seismic wave amplification and liquefaction potential. Extending rigorous validation to these complex geologies is critical for improving the reliability of numerical and machine learning models used in performance-based earthquake engineering and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Physics-Informed Neural Operators (PINOs), specifically DeepONet architectures, can be robustly validated against high-quality centrifuge experimental data for complex layered soil systems, and will demonstrate superior predictive accuracy and generalizability compared to purely data-driven models when trained on the extended LEAP validation framework.
proposals.method: We will leverage the established LEAP-2017 and LEAP-2022 experimental datasets as a baseline and extend the validation framework by incorporating new or re-analyzed centrifuge test data for layered sand-clay and variable saturation profiles. The core technical approach will employ a Physics-DeepONet architecture, integrating governing equations for porous media dynamics (u-p formulation) into the loss function. A stiffness-based loss term, informed by small-strain shear wave velocity profiles of each layer, will be added to constrain the operator learning. Bayesian optimization will be used to tune the hybrid loss weights and network hyperparameters for optimal performance on the complex deposit data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark validation case for advanced constitutive and surrogate models in geotechnical earthquake engineering. It will yield a publicly available, high-fidelity dataset and a validated PINO model capable of efficient and accurate seismic response prediction for realistic, non-uniform soil deposits, directly informing practice for critical infrastructure assessment.
proposals.novelty: While PINOs have been applied to structural dynamics and simple geotechnical problems, their rigorous validation within an international benchmark framework (LEAP) for complex soil systems is novel. The integration of layer-specific stiffness constraints into the DeepONet loss function for soil deposits represents a significant methodological advancement beyond prior physics-informed approaches.

proposals.title: A Structural Reliability Framework for Probabilistic Liquefaction Hazard Assessment: Quantifying Parametric and Model-Form Uncertainties
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction prediction is critical for seismic risk assessment but is plagued by significant uncertainties from soil parameters and the empirical or semi-empirical models themselves (e.g., SPT-, CPT-based). Current probabilistic approaches often treat model error simplistically, lacking a rigorous, integrated framework to decompose and propagate both parametric and model-form uncertainties into final performance estimates, hindering risk-informed decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting structural reliability methods—specifically First-Order Reliability Method (FORM) and Subset Simulation—within a Bayesian model averaging framework, we can systematically quantify and rank the contributions of parametric and model-form uncertainties to the total probability of liquefaction triggering, leading to more robust and interpretable hazard curves.
proposals.method: We will implement a nested reliability analysis. The outer loop will treat the selection among established liquefaction models (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss 2014, Robertson 2009) as a discrete model-form uncertainty, weighted via Bayesian model averaging using case history databases. Within each model, an inner loop will use FORM/Subset Simulation to propagate parametric uncertainties (e.g., (N1)60cs, CSR, magnitude scaling factor) through the limit-state function (FS=1). The Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture from the researcher's prior work will be adapted as a surrogate for expensive Monte Carlo simulations within the reliability analysis, ensuring computational efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified, computationally tractable framework for fully probabilistic liquefaction assessment that explicitly disaggregates uncertainty sources. It will provide practicing engineers with clearer insights into which uncertainties dominate hazard estimates and produce reliability-index-based liquefaction hazard curves that are more compatible with performance-based earthquake engineering methodologies.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of structural reliability algorithms (FORM/Subset Simulation) with Bayesian model averaging specifically for liquefaction, coupled with the use of a physics-informed operator network as a reliability analysis surrogate, moves beyond conventional logistic regression or simplified Monte Carlo approaches prevalent in pre-2026 geotechnical probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA).

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Validating Soil Constitutive Models via Element-Level Inversion of Centrifuge Data
proposals.motivation: Validating soil constitutive models for seismic analysis is critical for reliable geotechnical design, yet direct validation at the soil element level against realistic boundary conditions remains a challenge. High-quality centrifuge test data provides a rich, physics-consistent benchmark, but traditional methods struggle to inversely extract element-level stress-strain responses from complex soil-structure system data. This gap limits confidence in model predictions for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physics-informed neural operator framework, constrained by known system geometry and loading, can accurately invert centrifuge-measured system responses to recover validated, element-level constitutive behavior, thereby bridging the scale gap between laboratory element tests and integrated system performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, where the branch net encodes the centrifuge loading history and the trunk net encodes spatial coordinates. The network will be trained to map from boundary conditions to full-field system responses (e.g., displacements, accelerations). Crucially, a custom stiffness-based loss function, informed by candidate constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, MIT-S1), will regularize the inversion. A Bayesian optimization loop will then iteratively refine constitutive model parameters to minimize the discrepancy between the DeepONet-predicted and centrifuge-observed system responses, effectively validating the model at the element scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven methodology for the rigorous validation and calibration of advanced soil constitutive models using integrated system-level experiments. It will deliver an open-source computational tool for 'virtual' element testing, enhancing the reliability of numerical simulations in geotechnical earthquake engineering. The framework will also demonstrate a pathway for integrating physical experiments with explainable, physics-informed machine learning for model verification.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the system-level fidelity of centrifuge data with the granular validation target of element tests through inverse analysis using a physics-constrained neural operator, moving beyond conventional forward-model calibration. It integrates the researcher's recent work on Physics-Informed DeepONets and stiffness-based loss into a new inverse validation paradigm for geomechanics.

proposals.title: Full-Scale Levee Performance Prediction via Physics-Informed DeepONet with Multi-Fidelity Validation
proposals.motivation: Validating numerical models for large-scale earth structures like levees and embankments remains a critical, high-stakes challenge in civil engineering. Current practice relies heavily on costly and rare full-scale tests or simplified models that may not capture complex soil-structure interaction under extreme loads. A robust, validated predictive framework is urgently needed to improve the resilience assessment of critical infrastructure against earthquakes and floods.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed machine learning operator, trained on a hierarchy of data from centrifuge tests and high-fidelity numerical simulations, can accurately and efficiently predict the nonlinear, dynamic response of a full-scale levee system, overcoming the limitations of pure data-driven or conventional finite element approaches at this scale.
proposals.method: We will employ a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, extending the researcher's prior work, to learn the mapping from loading and material parameters to full-scale levee response fields (displacement, pore pressure). The model will be trained using a multi-fidelity dataset: 1) high-fidelity 3D FEM simulations of a full-scale levee, 2) data from well-instrumented centrifuge model tests (providing physical validation at reduced scale). A novel, stiffness-based loss function, incorporating soil constitutive models, will enforce physics constraints. Bayesian optimization will be used to select optimal sensor locations for virtual instrumentation of the full-scale numerical model to inform the training data strategy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable computational tool for full-scale earth structure analysis, directly bridging the gap between physical model tests and field-scale performance. It will provide a novel framework for integrating multi-fidelity data with physics-based machine learning, offering practitioners a more reliable and efficient method for infrastructure risk assessment and retrofit planning.
proposals.novelty: The integration of centrifuge test data as a physical constraint within a Physics-Informed DeepONet framework for full-scale prediction is novel, moving beyond purely numerical or empirical validation. This approach uniquely combines high-fidelity simulation, physical model data, and operator learning to tackle the scale-up problem in geotechnical engineering.

proposals.title: A Unified Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network for Static and Dynamic Soil Response Using a Single Constitutive Framework
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical practice often employs separate constitutive models for static (e.g., settlement) and dynamic (e.g., seismic) loading, leading to inefficiencies and potential inconsistencies in predicting soil-structure interaction. This separation is a significant limitation for infrastructure subjected to both slow deformation and rapid cyclic loads, such as bridges experiencing traffic and earthquakes. A unified modeling approach is needed to improve the accuracy and computational efficiency of integrated geotechnical analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Soil behavior under both static and dynamic loading regimes can be accurately captured by a single, physics-constrained constitutive model framework, where the apparent differences in response are governed by the rate-dependent evolution of internal state variables rather than fundamentally different mechanics.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet (PI-DeepONet) architecture where the branch net encodes loading paths (static vs. dynamic strain/time histories) and the trunk net evaluates at spatial/temporal coordinates. The core novelty is a unified, modular constitutive model layer, informed by rate-dependent plasticity theory (e.g., a generalized hypoplastic framework), integrated as a physics-based loss function. This stiffness-based loss will penalize deviations from thermodynamic constraints and known asymptotic behaviors (e.g., critical state), ensuring physical consistency. Bayesian optimization will be used to tune the hyperparameters of the network and the constitutive module for optimal generalization and explainability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, efficient computational tool that unifies static and dynamic geotechnical analysis, reducing model calibration effort and improving prediction consistency. It will advance the field of physics-informed machine learning by demonstrating how a single operator network can learn a generalized constitutive response. The resulting framework will provide clearer insights into the shared physical mechanisms governing soil deformation across loading rates.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a single constitutive model within a DeepONet architecture to simultaneously learn static and dynamic soil responses is unexplored. The approach moves beyond simply fitting separate models or using purely data-driven operators by enforcing a unified physics-based constitutive structure across fundamentally different loading regimes.

proposals.title: Long-term Validation and Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Seismic Site Amplification Assessment Across Diverse Geotechnical Environments
proposals.motivation: Current machine learning models for Seismic Site Amplification (SAA) prediction are typically validated on limited, short-term datasets, raising concerns about their long-term reliability and generalizability across diverse soil profiles and climatic conditions. For critical infrastructure design and risk assessment, a robust, validated SAA system that accounts for temporal effects like soil aging, seasonal moisture variation, and long-term degradation is essential. This study addresses the critical gap between short-term model performance and the long-term, real-world deployment required for resilient civil engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed machine learning framework, incorporating long-term monitoring data and soil-specific constitutive knowledge, will significantly outperform purely data-driven models in predicting SAA over multi-year timescales across diverse geotechnical environments. The system's accuracy will be maintained despite seasonal climatic cycles and evolving soil properties.
proposals.method: We will deploy a network of instrumented sites across diverse soil types (e.g., soft clay, liquefiable sand, stiff gravel) and climates for continuous, multi-year monitoring of ground motion and soil properties. The core predictive model will be a Physics-Informed DeepONet, building on the researcher's prior work, where the branch net encodes site-specific time-series data (e.g., pore pressure, shear wave velocity) and the trunk net encodes input functions (earthquake characteristics). The loss function will be augmented with stiffness-based regularization terms derived from long-term soil constitutive models and a novel temporal consistency penalty to ensure stability across seasons and years. Bayesian optimization will be used to tune hyperparameters for each site cluster.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, long-term, multi-site dataset for SAA validation, serving as a benchmark for the community. It will deliver a robust, physics-constrained SAA prediction framework explicitly validated for long-term reliability, directly informing performance-based earthquake engineering and code development. The study will also provide critical insights into the most influential time-dependent geotechnical parameters for amplification.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have applied Physics-Informed DeepONets to structural response, this proposal uniquely adapts and validates this architecture for long-term, site-specific geophysical forecasting. The integration of time-dependent soil mechanics directly into the loss function of a deep learning operator for multi-year SAA prediction is a novel synthesis of geotechnical engineering and machine learning.

proposals.title: Inverse Foundation Design via Physics-Informed DeepONets and Soil-Structure-Abstraction (SAA) Models for Target Seismic Performance
proposals.motivation: Current foundation design is largely a forward process, where a structure is analyzed for a given soil condition, often leading to iterative, costly adjustments to meet performance goals. This is inefficient for performance-based earthquake engineering, which aims to achieve specific, often non-uniform, deformation states. A direct inverse design methodology, which determines the optimal foundation geometry and properties for a prescribed soil-foundation interaction response, is critically needed to advance resilient and efficient infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed operator learning framework, integrating Soil-Structure-Abstraction (SAA) derived soil models, can be formulated to solve the inverse geotechnical design problem, reliably generating foundation designs that produce a target nonlinear soil response under seismic loading.
proposals.method: The method builds on the PI's prior work with Physics-Informed DeepONets and SAA models. First, a high-fidelity dataset of soil-foundation systems will be generated using validated numerical models. A novel Inverse-Physics-Informed DeepONet (I-PI-DeepONet) will be trained, where the branch net encodes the target soil reaction force-displacement or pore pressure time history, and the trunk net encodes spatial/temporal coordinates. The forward physics (e.g., equilibrium, constitutive relations) will be embedded as stiffness-based and momentum conservation loss terms, regularizing the ill-posed inverse problem. Bayesian optimization will then be used to efficiently search the design space (e.g., pile dimensions, layout) for configurations that minimize the discrepancy between the target and I-PI-DeepONet-predicted response.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for the inverse design of foundations, directly linking target seismic performance metrics to constructible design parameters. It will advance the field of performance-based geotechnical design by providing a rigorous, physics-constrained data-driven tool that reduces reliance on trial-and-error analysis. The resulting methodology will be a significant step towards automated, optimization-driven civil infrastructure design.
proposals.novelty: While DeepONets have been used for forward simulation, their application to the inverse design of geotechnical systems, explicitly conditioned on target soil response and constrained by SAA-model physics, is a novel integration. This moves beyond response prediction to automated generative design for soil-structure interaction problems.

proposals.title: Inverse Inference of Traffic and Seismic Loading Histories from Residual Soil Deformations using Physics-Informed Neural Operators
proposals.motivation: Critical infrastructure is subject to cumulative damage from repeated traffic loads and seismic events, which is often not directly monitored. Current Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) focuses on the structure, not the soil-foundation system. The soil's residual deformations, captured via Shape Acceleration Arrays (SAAs), hold a unique, untapped record of past loading, offering a novel pathway for forensic engineering and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The residual deformation field in soil, measured by embedded SAAs, contains sufficient information to inversely infer the magnitude, type (e.g., traffic vs. seismic), and approximate temporal sequence of past loading events through a physics-constrained machine learning framework.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, extending the researcher's prior work, to learn the mapping from observed residual soil deformation profiles (output function) to probable loading histories (input function). The network will be trained on synthetic data generated from high-fidelity, nonlinear finite element models of soil-structure interaction under stochastic traffic and seismic sequences. A stiffness-based loss function, informed by soil constitutive models, will regularize the ill-posed inverse problem. Bayesian optimization will be used to efficiently explore the high-dimensional parameter space of possible load histories.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, data-driven inverse analysis tool for forensic geotechnical engineering, enabling the reconstruction of unrecorded loading events. It will advance the field of physics-informed machine learning by applying it to a challenging inverse problem in civil engineering, shifting SHM paradigms from direct measurement to inference from soil memory.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses SAAs for forward prediction or event detection, this proposal inverts the problem: using final soil state to infer past loading sequences. It uniquely combines the researcher's expertise in physics-informed DeepONets for structures with geotechnical inverse analysis, a largely unexplored synergy.

proposals.title: Apparatus-Specific Calibration of Cone Penetration Test Systems via Inverse Soil Replication and High-G Penetration Testing
proposals.motivation: The Cone Penetration Test (CPT) is a critical tool for geotechnical site characterization, but its accuracy is compromised by apparatus-specific systematic errors, including sleeve friction and dynamic rod effects. These errors are not accounted for in current empirical correlations, leading to uncertainties in soil property interpretation and structural foundation design. A physics-based, apparatus-specific calibration framework is needed to transform CPT data into a more reliable 'ground truth' measurement.
proposals.hypothesis: By using an inverse soil modeling procedure to physically replicate in-situ soil states inferred from CPT data, and then performing controlled penetration tests with multiple CPT rigs, the systematic errors inherent to each specific apparatus can be isolated, quantified, and corrected.
proposals.method: The research will employ a physics-informed machine learning framework. First, a Physics-Informed DeepONet, pre-trained on high-fidelity numerical simulations, will be used as the inverse operator to predict soil preparation parameters (e.g., density, stress state) from field CPT traces (qc, fs). A centrifuge testing program will then create these 'target' soil specimens. Multiple CPT systems, including different rig designs and data acquisition setups, will perform penetration tests in the replicated soil under identical high-g conditions. A Bayesian optimization explainable AI (XAI) approach will analyze the resulting multi-apparatus dataset to isolate and model the functional form of systematic errors for each rig.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel, physically-grounded methodology for apparatus-specific CPT calibration, moving beyond statistical black-box corrections. 2) A publicly available benchmark dataset of high-fidelity soil replication paired with multi-rig penetration responses. 3) A prototype 'digital twin' calibration framework that can be applied to field data to reduce interpretation uncertainty for critical infrastructure projects.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of inverse soil state modeling for physical replication with comparative multi-apparatus testing creates a closed-loop, ground-truth-verified system for error quantification, a paradigm shift from current statistical or numerical calibration studies that lack physical validation.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework to Establish CPT as the Primary Measurement for In-Situ Soil Properties
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical practice treats laboratory tests on disturbed samples as the 'gold standard' for soil properties, despite well-documented sampling and handling errors that bias results. The Cone Penetration Test (CPT) provides high-resolution, continuous, and repeatable in-situ data but is relegated to a secondary, correlative role. This paradigm limits the accuracy and reliability of soil models for critical civil infrastructure design and seismic site response analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the calibration hierarchy, a CPT profile can be established as the primary, high-fidelity measurement of in-situ state, with all other data (e.g., laboratory tests, SPT) used to interpret its continuous signal through a physics-constrained learning framework.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed Neural Operator (PINO) architecture, specifically a DeepONet variant, trained to map raw CPT traces (qc, fs, u2) to fundamental soil properties (e.g., relative density, friction angle, small-strain stiffness). The model will be constrained by governing mechanics (e.g., cavity expansion, critical state theory) via custom stiffness-based loss functions. A Bayesian optimization and explainable AI (XAI) pipeline, akin to the researcher's prior work, will be used to optimally select sparse, targeted laboratory tests for calibration, validating the operator's predictions and quantifying uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-centric theoretical framework that redefines the role of CPT in geotechnical characterization. It will deliver an open-source, validated physics-informed operator for direct property derivation from CPT, reducing reliance on disturbed samples and creating a more robust link between in-situ measurement and structural design models.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the paradigm inversion itself—treating CPT as the primary signal rather than a correlative tool—implemented through a hybrid physics-informed neural operator that integrates continuous field data with sparse lab data in a fundamentally new calibration hierarchy.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed DeepONet Framework for CPT-Based Unified Soil Classification and State Parameter Estimation from Centrifuge Data
proposals.motivation: The Cone Penetration Test (CPT) is a rapid, continuous in-situ tool, but its interpretation for complex soils (e.g., silty sands, layered profiles, cohesive clays) remains ambiguous, relying heavily on empirical, soil-type-specific correlations. This limits its use as a primary tool for defining fundamental state parameters like relative density, stress state, and undrained shear strength, creating uncertainty in geotechnical design and analysis. A unified, physics-grounded framework is needed to directly extract these primary parameters from CPT data across diverse soil conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physics-informed machine learning operator, trained on high-fidelity centrifuge CPT data from a strategically designed suite of complex soil models, can learn a unified, soil-type-agnostic mapping from raw CPT traces (qc, fs, u2) to fundamental soil state parameters and a consistent classification index.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of instrumented centrifuge tests on prepared models of complex soils (silty sands, layered sand-clay profiles, and normally-to-overconsolidated clays). A new, instrumented CPT will provide high-resolution data. We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, where the branch net encodes the CPT input trace and the trunk net encodes spatial depth. The physics-informed loss will incorporate simplified cavity expansion and critical state soil mechanics constraints, regularizing the network to learn physically plausible mappings from CPT resistance to state parameters like relative density, friction angle, and OCR.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, interpretable computational framework that directly inverts CPT data for primary soil state parameters, moving beyond empirical classification charts. It will provide a robust, generalizable tool for site characterization, enhancing the reliability of seismic site response and liquefaction analyses. The centrifuge-validated dataset will serve as a benchmark for future CPT interpretation methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of controlled centrifuge physical modeling with a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture to create a unified, physics-constrained inversion model. This moves beyond purely data-driven or soil-specific empirical correlations, aiming for a generalizable operator that embeds mechanistic understanding directly into the CPT interpretation process for complex soils.

proposals.title: An Adaptive Physics-Informed Control Framework for Real-Time Subset Analysis in Centrifuge Modeling of Soil Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge testing is the gold standard for simulating complex soil-structure interaction under seismic loading, but it remains largely a passive, post-hoc analysis tool. Critical transient phenomena, such as the precise onset of liquefaction or the dissipation of excess pore pressures, are often identified only after the experiment, missing opportunities for real-time, targeted investigation. An adaptive system that can autonomously detect and focus on these key phases would revolutionize experimental efficiency and data yield.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time piezocone pore pressure data with an adaptive, physics-informed control system will enable the automatic identification and isolation of critical geotechnical event subsets (e.g., pre-failure accumulation, post-liquefaction dissipation), leading to more efficient and insightful centrifuge experiments focused on the most consequential soil behavior.
proposals.method: We will integrate a 6mm piezocone with high-frequency data acquisition into an existing centrifuge model container. The core adaptive controller will be built using a physics-informed DeepONet architecture, pre-trained on synthetic data from coupled hydro-mechanical simulations, to predict near-future pore pressure trends. A secondary Bayesian optimization explainable machine learning (BO-XML) model, similar to the PI's prior work, will be used to define and dynamically update critical thresholds (e.g., ru ratio) that trigger the system to flag a data subset and initiate pre-programmed, high-resolution analysis protocols for that specific phase.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, closed-loop centrifuge testing paradigm that shifts from passive recording to active, intelligent investigation. It will provide a new methodology for extracting maximum insight from costly physical models by ensuring computational and analytical resources are concentrated on the most geotechnically significant instants. The resulting high-fidelity subset data will be invaluable for validating and improving constitutive models for liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of real-time physical sensor feedback with a physics-informed machine learning control system to create an 'active' centrifuge experiment that autonomously reconfigures its own analysis focus, a significant departure from static testing protocols and post-experiment data mining.

proposals.title: Federated Physics-Informed Learning for High-Resolution Soil Constitutive Modeling from Distributed MEMS-CPT Data
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge testing provides vital data for soil-structure interaction, but data sharing is limited by physical logistics and proprietary concerns, hindering the development of robust, generalizable constitutive models. Concurrently, the integration of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) into Cone Penetration Test (CPT) cones offers unprecedented spatial resolution of tip and sleeve stresses, generating rich but localized datasets. A framework is needed to leverage these distributed, high-fidelity datasets collaboratively without compromising data sovereignty.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated learning framework, aggregating locally trained physics-informed neural operator models from distributed MEMS-CPT data, can produce a globally calibrated soil constitutive model that outperforms models trained on single-facility data and preserves site-specific variability.
proposals.method: We will develop automated protocols to standardize MEMS-CPT data from multiple centrifuge facilities. At each node, a local Physics-Informed DeepONet, incorporating stiffness-based loss functions from Abdoun's prior work, will be trained on its high-resolution stress-strain data. Instead of sharing raw data, only the model parameter updates (gradients) from these local models will be securely aggregated using a federated averaging (FedAvg) algorithm on a central server to iteratively refine a global model. This federated training loop will explicitly enforce physics constraints at both local and global levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, privacy-preserving computational framework for collaborative geotechnical model development. It will yield a high-fidelity, universally calibrated constitutive model informed by previously inaccessible distributed experimental data. The methodology will also provide a blueprint for federated learning in experimental mechanics, addressing data heterogeneity and sovereignty.
proposals.novelty: This is the first proposal to fuse federated learning with physics-informed neural operators for geotechnical sensing, uniquely addressing data sovereignty while leveraging ultra-high-resolution MEMS-CPT data. It extends automated data protocols beyond comparison to enable collaborative, distributed model training without centralizing sensitive experimental data.

proposals.title: Adversarially Learned Probabilistic Soil Genesis Manifold from Cone Penetration Test Data
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical practice treats the Cone Penetration Test (CPT) as a direct site investigation tool, but the inverse problem—determining the soil preparation history from a CPT trace—is critical for laboratory validation of field performance and constitutive model calibration. This inverse problem is fundamentally ill-posed, as multiple soil states can produce similar CPT signatures, yet existing deterministic or simple regression approaches fail to quantify this non-uniqueness. A framework that maps a CPT trace to a constrained set of plausible, lab-replicable soil genesis parameters is urgently needed for robust experimental design and risk-informed decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial learning framework, trained on a curated dataset of CPT-metadata pairs from controlled laboratory preparations, can learn the high-dimensional, probabilistic mapping from a CPT signature to a manifold of feasible soil genesis parameters (e.g., gradation, compaction, moisture). This manifold will effectively capture the inherent non-uniqueness of the inverse problem while constraining solutions to physically realistic and laboratory-achievable states.
proposals.method: We will curate a multi-laboratory dataset linking detailed soil preparation metadata to corresponding CPT traces from calibration chambers. A conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN) architecture will be implemented, where the generator maps a CPT input vector and a latent noise vector to a set of genesis parameters, and the discriminator evaluates the plausibility of the (CPT, genesis) pair. The training objective will be augmented with physics-informed regularization terms, drawing on pre-2026 Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) concepts, to penalize genesis parameters that violate basic geotechnical principles (e.g., unrealistic porosity for a given compaction energy). The trained generator will produce a distribution of outputs for a single CPT trace, defining the genesis space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first adversarial learning-based tool for probabilistically inverting CPT data into a space of possible soil preparation states, directly supporting high-fidelity laboratory specimen replication. It will advance the field by moving beyond single-point estimates to deliver explicit, quantifiable uncertainty on inferred soil genesis parameters. The output genesis manifold will serve as a critical input for probabilistic ground response analysis and structural fragility assessment, enhancing the realism of seismic performance evaluations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing the CPT inverse problem from a deterministic regression to the adversarial learning of a constrained probabilistic manifold, explicitly designed to capture non-unique solutions. This integrates the cGAN framework—common in image synthesis pre-2026—with domain-specific physics-based regularization for geotechnical engineering, a novel combination not yet explored in soil characterization.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Laboratory Soil Specimens via Cone Penetration Test Signatures and a Physics-Informed Generative Framework
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical physical modeling and calibration chamber testing require precise replication of in-situ soil conditions, a process currently reliant on empirical trial-and-error. This hinders the reliability and reproducibility of experimental studies. Adapting the Materials Genome Initiative's inverse design paradigm to geotechnics offers a transformative, data-driven solution to this long-standing 'soil fabrication' challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A comprehensive database linking high-resolution CPT data to precise soil preparation recipes, when processed by a physics-informed inverse model, can accurately predict the laboratory procedures needed to replicate a target soil fabric and state from its CPT signature.
proposals.method: We will compile a multi-laboratory database of CPT traces from manufactured specimens, each paired with a detailed procedural 'genome' (PSD, compaction energy, water content, placement method). A conditional generative model, such as a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) or a Physics-Informed Generative Adversarial Network (PI-GAN), will be developed. The model will be trained to invert the CPT-soil property relationship, using physics-based constraints (e.g., from critical state soil mechanics) within the loss function to ensure geotechnical plausibility of the generated fabrication recipes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, algorithm-driven framework for the inverse design of laboratory soil specimens, directly linking in-situ test data to fabrication protocols. It will enhance the fidelity of physical modeling and provide a novel benchmark for validating constitutive models against precisely replicated field conditions.
proposals.novelty: This represents the first application of the materials inverse design 'genome' concept to geomaterials, moving beyond forward property prediction. The integration of generative AI with geotechnical physics for solving the soil fabrication inverse problem is a significant departure from current empirical methods.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Joint Inversion of CPT and Electromagnetic Induction Data for 3D Soil Characterization
proposals.motivation: Accurate subsurface characterization is critical for geotechnical engineering, yet current methods face a trade-off between direct, high-resolution point measurements (e.g., CPT) and indirect, spatially extensive surveys (e.g., EMI). This limits the development of comprehensive 3D site models needed for reliable seismic risk assessment and infrastructure design. A data fusion framework, analogous to geophysical joint inversion, is required to integrate these complementary data types efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed joint inversion framework, which fuses direct Cone Penetration Test (CPT) data with surface-based Electromagnetic Induction (EMI) measurements, can generate a more accurate and spatially comprehensive 3D model of soil mechanical and compositional properties than either method alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage data fusion pipeline. First, a physics-informed DeepONet architecture will be trained to learn the mapping between soil stratigraphy/mechanical properties (from CPT) and bulk electrical conductivity (from EMI) at co-located soundings. This learned operator will then serve as a core constraint in a Bayesian joint inversion framework. The CPT data will provide high-fidelity priors and calibration points, while the EMI data will provide the spatial coverage. The inversion will be regularized using known physical relationships between soil strength, density, and electrical conductivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical framework for generating integrated 3D subsurface models that combine mechanical and compositional properties, directly addressing a key gap in site investigation. It will advance the application of physics-informed machine learning operators for geotechnical data fusion, providing a scalable template for integrating other sensor pairs.
proposals.novelty: While joint inversion is standard in geophysics, its application to fuse direct intrusive CPT data with surface-based EMI for geotechnical engineering is novel. The core innovation is the use of a physics-informed DeepONet as the constitutive operator within a Bayesian inversion, moving beyond simple correlation to embed physical relationships directly into the fusion process.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network for Probabilistic Seismic Assessment Using Conditional Spectrum-Matched Ground Motion Suites
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic engineering relies on simplified ground motion selection, often using a limited number of records or artificial signals, which can lead to biased and non-generalizable structural response predictions. The Conditional Spectrum (CS) method provides a rigorous, hazard-consistent framework for ground motion selection, but its probabilistic application to complex structural systems remains computationally prohibitive for routine engineering practice. This gap limits the robust implementation of risk-targeted design and assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network (DeepONet) with a stiffness-based loss function can efficiently and accurately emulate the probabilistic structural response to large suites of CS-matched ground motions, enabling a full probabilistic seismic assessment that is both computationally tractable and physically consistent.
proposals.method: We will generate a large, site-specific dataset of structural responses by subjecting high-fidelity finite element models of representative civil structures (e.g., soil-foundation-structure systems) to suites of CS-matched ground motions. A DeepONet architecture will be trained to learn the mapping from ground motion time histories to structural response quantities of interest (e.g., inter-story drift, damage indices). The network's physics-informed loss function will incorporate stiffness-based regularization terms derived from the structural model's known properties, ensuring physical plausibility and improving extrapolation. Bayesian optimization will be used to tune the hyperparameters of the DeepONet training process for optimal performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, efficient computational surrogate that directly links site-specific seismic hazard (via the CS) to probabilistic structural performance metrics, advancing performance-based earthquake engineering. It will provide a practical tool for engineers to conduct robust risk assessments without prohibitive computational cost, directly building upon the PI's expertise in physics-informed machine learning for structural dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the rigorous, hazard-consistent Conditional Spectrum framework with a physics-constrained DeepONet for probabilistic seismic response emulation, moving beyond deterministic or small-suite analyses to a fully probabilistic, yet efficient, assessment paradigm.

proposals.title: A Library of Characteristic Seismic Motifs for Realistic and Efficient Structural Performance Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current structural analysis and educational shake-table testing often rely on oversimplified synthetic motions (e.g., ramped sine waves), which fail to capture the complex, damaging characteristics of real earthquakes. This leads to inaccurate predictions of structural damage and non-conservative designs, a critical issue in seismic engineering. A more realistic, yet standardized, set of base motions is needed to improve the reliability of performance assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: That a curated library of fundamental, data-derived earthquake signatures (seismic motifs) can effectively replace simplified synthetic motions, leading to more accurate and generalizable predictions of structural response and damage compared to conventional methods.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze a large database of recorded ground motions (e.g., from PEER NGA-West2). Using unsupervised machine learning techniques, specifically a physics-informed clustering algorithm (e.g., a custom kernel combining time and frequency domain features) and dimensionality reduction (t-SNE), we will identify recurring patterns or 'motifs'. Each motif will be validated and characterized by its geotechnical and source parameters. The final library will be integrated into a Physics-Informed DeepONet framework, where the operator learns to map from a selected motif and structural parameters to the dynamic response, using stiffness-based loss functions for physical consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source library of realistic, characteristic earthquake motions for analysis and testing, moving beyond simplistic synthetic waves. It will deliver a new, efficient workflow for structural response prediction that is grounded in real seismic data, thereby improving the accuracy of seismic vulnerability assessments and the educational value of physical model tests.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogy to structural biology's 'folding motif' concept for standardizing seismic input, and in the integration of unsupervised seismic motif discovery with physics-informed neural operators (DeepONet) for structural response prediction, a synthesis not yet explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Active Learning for Intelligent Data Subsetting in Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing of Civil Infrastructure
proposals.motivation: Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing (DFOS), particularly Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) arrays, offers unprecedented spatial resolution for structural health monitoring (SHM) but generates overwhelming data streams. Current methods struggle to efficiently identify critical events or damage precursors within this data deluge, leading to wasted storage, computational burden, and delayed diagnosis. An intelligent framework is needed to automatically pinpoint the most informative spatial-temporal subsets for detailed analysis, enabling proactive and resource-efficient monitoring of civil structures.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid framework combining physics-informed neural operators with anomaly detection algorithms can autonomously and accurately identify critical subsets of DFOS data that correspond to anomalous structural behavior, complex loading events, or incipient damage, thereby transforming continuous monitoring into an active, hypothesis-driven process.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet, pre-trained on finite element simulations of instrumented structural elements (e.g., piles, rods) under various loads, to establish a baseline for 'normal' operational response. This operator will be integrated with an unsupervised learning module (e.g., an Isolation Forest or autoencoder) to detect deviations in real FBG strain/temperature data streams. A Bayesian optimization layer will then guide the selection of optimal spatial (sensor segments) and temporal (time windows) subsets for focused analysis, prioritizing data that maximizes information gain regarding structural state.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, automated workflow for SHM that shifts from passive data logging to active, context-aware monitoring. It will provide a concrete methodology for drastically reducing data volume without losing critical information, enabling faster and more precise damage diagnosis. The framework will be validated on experimental FBG-instrumented geotechnical or structural specimens, demonstrating its utility for civil engineering applications.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a physics-informed neural operator for baseline modeling with an active learning loop for intelligent subset selection in DFOS data is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond standard anomaly detection by using a physics-constrained model to define normality and a decision-making process to curate high-value experimental subsets for engineers.

proposals.title: A Geotechnical Response Portfolio: A Multi-Metric Correlation Framework for Holistic System Performance Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical monitoring and analysis often rely on single or dual-metric thresholds (e.g., peak acceleration, maximum displacement) to assess system performance and predict failure. This approach can miss critical interdependencies between different physical response indicators, leading to incomplete risk assessments and missed early warning signs. A more holistic, multi-metric framework is needed to capture the complex, correlated pre-failure behavior of soil-structure systems, analogous to how financial portfolios are managed using a suite of correlated risk metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: The correlated evolution of a defined portfolio of geotechnical response metrics—such as shear wave velocity, acoustic emission rate, distributed strain energy density, and the temporal derivative of the factor of safety—contains predictive information about system state and impending failure that is not accessible through any single metric in isolation.
proposals.method: We will define a geotechnical response portfolio vector for a system using data from centrifuge tests and field monitoring. Correlation structures (e.g., time-varying covariance matrices) and cross-metric sensitivities will be analyzed using techniques adapted from financial econometrics, such as Dynamic Conditional Correlation (DCC-GARCH) models and Principal Component Analysis (PCA). A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) or a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture will then be trained to learn the mapping from initial conditions and loading to the portfolio's correlated evolution, with the portfolio correlation loss regularized by known physical constraints (e.g., energy balance).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systematic framework for multi-sensor data fusion in geotechnical health monitoring, moving beyond univariate analysis. It will deliver a new class of correlated, physics-informed precursor indicators for system instability, enhancing early warning capabilities for slopes, foundations, and retaining structures under dynamic loading.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal adaptation of multi-metric portfolio analysis from quantitative finance to geotechnical engineering, creating an integrated 'response portfolio' concept. This is distinct from existing multi-sensor studies as it explicitly models the time-evolving correlations between disparate physical metrics as a primary source of diagnostic information.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Inter-Facility Centrifuge Discrepancies via Physics-Informed Correlation Matrix Analysis
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge testing is a cornerstone of physical modeling for soil-structure interaction and seismic performance, yet results from nominally identical tests can vary across facilities. These discrepancies undermine confidence in validation data for numerical models and design codes. Current practice often focuses on comparing individual response metrics, which fails to identify the root causes of divergence, such as subtle differences in boundary conditions, model construction, or sensor interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: Different systematic sources of inter-facility discrepancy (e.g., flexible vs. rigid container boundaries, variations in soil saturation procedures, or sensor calibration drifts) will produce distinct, identifiable perturbations in the correlation matrices of multi-metric physical responses, serving as a diagnostic signature for the underlying mechanism.
proposals.method: We will compile datasets from replicated centrifuge tests (e.g., LEAP or VELACS analogs) across multiple facilities, extracting time-series for accelerations, pore pressures, strains, and bending moments. Correlation matrices will be computed for each test. A physics-informed Bayesian optimization framework, building on the researcher's prior work, will be used to map perturbations in these correlation structures (e.g., changes in correlation strength between specific sensor pairs) to candidate discrepancy sources. The analysis will be guided by stiffness-based loss functions to ensure physical plausibility in the diagnosed mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, data-driven framework for root-cause analysis of centrifuge discrepancies, moving beyond qualitative comparisons. It will deliver a diagnostic tool for facilities to improve test protocols and enable more robust meta-analysis of distributed experimental data, strengthening the validation basis for computational models in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While correlation analysis is common, its application as a structured diagnostic tool for physical model discrepancies is novel. The integration of correlation matrix perturbations with a physics-informed optimization scheme to pinpoint specific experimental error sources represents a significant advance over conventional single-metric comparisons.

proposals.title: A Tiered Adversarial Validation Framework for Soil Constitutive Models Using Physics-Informed Machine Learning and Blind Tests
proposals.motivation: Current calibration of advanced soil constitutive models (e.g., SANISAND, MIT-SR) often relies on curve-fitting to a single dataset, conflating calibration with validation and leading to poor predictive performance in novel stress paths or boundary conditions. This overfitting poses significant risks in geotechnical engineering design, where models must extrapolate reliably for seismic analysis and performance-based design. A rigorous, standardized validation protocol is critically needed to assess true model generalizability.
proposals.hypothesis: A tiered validation framework, which strategically partitions experimental data for calibration and adversarial blind testing, will quantitatively reveal the extrapolative limits of constitutive models. We hypothesize that models passing this adversarial test will demonstrate significantly lower predictive uncertainty in real-world boundary-value simulations.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured database from high-quality element tests (triaxial, cyclic simple shear) on sands and clays, partitioning it into Tier 1 (calibration), Tier 2 (interpolation validation), and Tier 3 (adversarial blind tests probing novel stress paths). Model calibration will employ a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, integrating known thermodynamic constraints as stiffness-based loss functions to ensure physical plausibility. Final validation will use quantitative metrics (e.g., normalized RMSE, uncertainty bounds via Bayesian deep learning) on the withheld Tier 3 data to assess predictive accuracy beyond calibration domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, open-source protocol for the rigorous predictive validation of soil constitutive models, moving the field beyond qualitative curve-matching.
