It will deliver a publicly available benchmark dataset with predefined validation tiers and establish quantitative performance thresholds for model acceptance in engineering practice, directly enhancing reliability in seismic site response and structural interaction analyses.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adversarial design of the blind test tier (Tier 3) specifically to challenge model boundaries, combined with the use of physics-informed machine learning not for surrogate modeling, but as a physically-constrained tool for robust parameter identification and uncertainty quantification during the calibration phase.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for the Unified Calibration and Validation of Constitutive Models for Partially Saturated Slopes
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures are a major geotechnical hazard, where failure is governed by transient, spatially varying saturation. Pre-2026, numerical models for slope stability often rely on constitutive models calibrated for either fully saturated or dry conditions, creating a significant predictive gap for the critical partially saturated state. A unified model capable of seamless transition across saturation degrees is essential for accurate risk assessment of infrastructure and communities.
proposals.hypothesis: A constitutive modeling framework, extended from the Barcelona Basic Model (BBM) and calibrated against a comprehensive experimental dataset spanning dry to saturated states, can be significantly enhanced and validated using physics-informed machine learning to accurately predict the dynamic response and stability of slopes under transient infiltration.
proposals.method: We will first generate a high-quality experimental dataset via triaxial and direct shear tests on a silty sand under controlled suction (from dry to submerged). This data will be used to calibrate an extended BBM framework. The core technical innovation is the integration of this physics-based model with a Physics-Informed Neural Operator (PINO) architecture or a tailored Physics-Infused DeepONet. The neural operator will be trained to learn the mapping from initial conditions and infiltration boundary conditions to the slope's dynamic response, using the constitutive model to inform its loss function, ensuring predictions adhere to fundamental soil mechanics principles across all saturation levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, experimentally validated numerical tool for simulating slope failure under realistic, transient hydrological loading. It will advance the field by providing a unified constitutive modeling approach that bridges saturated and unsaturated soil mechanics. The integration of physics-infused machine learning will create a new, efficient paradigm for geotechnical boundary value problem analysis, directly supporting more reliable infrastructure design and hazard mitigation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in just calibrating a model with varied data, but in fusing the extended BBM constitutive framework with a physics-informed neural operator architecture specifically to handle the dynamic transitions between saturation states, creating a hybrid model that is both physically consistent and computationally efficient for full-scale slope simulations.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Validating and Calibrating Advanced Soil Constitutive Models
proposals.motivation: Accurate numerical simulation of soil-structure interaction under dynamic loads, such as earthquakes, relies heavily on the fidelity of soil constitutive models. However, validating and calibrating these complex models against varied experimental data (e.g., centrifuge tests, cyclic triaxial tests) remains a manual, time-intensive process with significant uncertainty. An automated, data-driven framework is needed to rigorously quantify model-form errors and improve predictive reliability for geotechnical seismic analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating physics-informed machine learning operators with a Bayesian optimization framework can systematically validate, calibrate, and quantify the uncertainty of advanced soil constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) against a multi-fidelity experimental dataset, leading to more reliable and computationally efficient numerical predictions.
proposals.method: We will leverage a curated experimental dataset (centrifuge, element tests) as ground truth. A Physics-Informed DeepONet, incorporating stiffness-based loss functions as in the researcher's prior work, will be trained to act as a high-fidelity surrogate for soil response. This surrogate will be used within an integrated Bayesian optimization (BO) loop to calibrate the parameters of traditional constitutive models by minimizing the discrepancy between model outputs and experimental data. The explainable AI (XAI) components from the researcher's prior amplification study will be adapted to interpret the BO results, identifying which model parameters and physical mechanisms most influence validation outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, automated pipeline for constitutive model validation and calibration, directly linking experimental data to numerical practice. It will provide a quantitative measure of model-form error for common soil models under seismic conditions. The resulting calibrated models and open-source framework will enable more trustworthy seismic site response and soil-structure interaction analyses for practicing engineers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a physics-informed DeepONet surrogate within a Bayesian optimization loop for geotechnical model validation, moving beyond simple parameter fitting to a holistic assessment of model physics against data. This combines the researcher's expertise in physics-informed operators and explainable Bayesian methods into a new application for constitutive modeling.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Bayesian Operational Modal Analysis Framework for Probabilistic Slope Stability Monitoring Using Ambient Vibrations
proposals.motivation: Slope failures pose significant geotechnical hazards, but current monitoring methods often rely on point measurements (e.g., inclinometers) or surface displacements, which may provide late-stage warnings. Pre-2026 advances in structural health monitoring (SHM) demonstrate that changes in a system's dynamic properties are sensitive precursors to damage. Adapting these output-only, vibration-based techniques to geotechnical systems offers a promising, non-invasive method for continuous, area-wide stability assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The evolution of a soil slope's modal parameters (natural frequencies, damping ratios, and mode shapes), identified from its ambient vibration response and updated within a Bayesian framework, provides a statistically robust, early-warning indicator of incipient slope failure before large deformations occur.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense array of seismometers on a test slope for continuous ambient vibration recording. Data will be processed using Stochastic Subspace Identification (SSI) for robust operational modal analysis in the time domain. The identified modal parameters (with associated uncertainty) will serve as observational data for Bayesian updating of a simplified physics-based slope model (e.g., a shear beam or finite element model). The updating will use sequential Monte Carlo methods or Markov Chain Monte Carlo to track the posterior distribution of soil stiffness parameters over time, quantifying probabilistic trends indicative of weakening.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for slope stability monitoring by fusing geotechnical engineering with advanced SHM techniques. It will deliver a probabilistic, model-updating framework that quantifies uncertainty in stability assessments, moving beyond deterministic thresholds. The outcome is a directly applicable early-warning system prototype based on dynamic system identification.
proposals.novelty: The integration of output-only Operational Modal Analysis (common in structural engineering) with Bayesian updating for geotechnical slope monitoring is a significant cross-disciplinary innovation. Furthermore, incorporating the researcher's expertise in physics-informed machine learning to regularize the modal identification or the Bayesian inverse problem distinguishes it from purely data-driven approaches.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Site-Specific Slope Transfer Function Estimation Using Controlled Seismic Excitation
proposals.motivation: Current seismic slope stability assessments often rely on simplified 1D site response analyses or costly and invasive subsurface investigations, which may not capture the complex 3D wave propagation and internal heterogeneity of natural slopes. A direct, empirical method to characterize a slope's dynamic transfer function would provide a critical fingerprint for its internal structure and health, directly informing vulnerability to seismic loading. This is essential for resilient infrastructure design and proactive hazard mitigation.
proposals.hypothesis: That advanced signal processing techniques from audio system identification, when combined with physics-informed machine learning, can robustly extract high-fidelity, site-specific slope transfer functions from controlled seismic excitation and response measurements. These transfer functions will contain identifiable features correlating with internal soil layering, stiffness contrasts, and potential weak planes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Maximum Length Sequence (MLS) and exponential sine sweep excitation methods, using a vibroseis source to inject a known, broadband seismic signal at a slope's base or crest. A dense surface array of geophones will record the response. The core processing will use iterative time-domain deconvolution and spectral division to compute initial impulse responses. A Physics-Informed Neural Operator (PINO) architecture, trained with a combined data loss and stiffness/damping regularization loss derived from wave equation constraints, will then refine these estimates, denoise the data, and provide a continuous, interpretable transfer function model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, non-invasive field-testing and analysis protocol for empirical slope dynamic characterization. It will produce a new class of physics-consistent slope 'fingerprints' that can directly calibrate or update numerical models for seismic analysis. The framework contributes to the emerging paradigm of physics-informed data-driven site characterization in geotechnical engineering.
proposals.novelty: The direct cross-disciplinary adaptation of high-precision audio/acoustic system identification protocols to field-scale geotechnical problems is novel. Furthermore, integrating this with a physics-informed neural operator to constrain the solution with wave propagation physics moves beyond pure black-box system identification, ensuring results are physically plausible and interpretable for engineers.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed DeepONet Framework for System Identification of Soil Slope Dynamic Transfer Functions
proposals.motivation: Predicting the seismic response of soil slopes is critical for geotechnical risk assessment, yet current models often rely on simplified soil profiles or purely data-driven methods that lack physical consistency. System identification (SI) from structural dynamics offers a powerful framework to characterize a slope's dynamic transfer function from input-output field data, but its application to complex, heterogeneous geotechnical systems remains limited. This gap hinders accurate site-specific amplification and deformation predictions for slope stability analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed neural operator framework, integrating domain knowledge as soft constraints, can robustly identify the dynamic transfer function of a soil slope from sparse seismic input-output measurements, outperforming purely data-driven SI methods in generalization and physical interpretability.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet (PI-DeepONet) architecture, where the branch net processes input ground motion time histories and the trunk net evaluates spatiotemporal coordinates. The network will be trained to map seismic inputs to slope response outputs (e.g., accelerations at depth). Physics will be embedded via custom stiffness-based and wave propagation loss functions, regularizing the learning. The trained operator's latent representation will be analyzed to extract the slope's frequency-dependent transfer function, with uncertainty quantified using a Bayesian optimization wrapper for hyperparameter tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-constrained SI tool specifically for geotechnical systems, enabling more reliable characterization of site amplification for slopes. It will advance the field by providing a methodology to derive interpretable, dynamic soil models directly from field data, bridging the gap between geotechnical site response analysis and structural dynamics SI techniques.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored integration of physics-informed neural operators (specifically DeepONet) with system identification for soil slopes, moving beyond applications to fixed structures. This approach uniquely constrains the ill-posed inverse problem of slope SI using geotechnical principles within a flexible operator learning framework.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed DeepONet Framework for Element-Level Cyclic Soil Behavior: From Simple Shear Tests to Generalized Constitutive Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current data-driven models for seismic soil response often rely on site-specific field data or complex centrifuge tests, which conflate soil constitutive behavior with boundary effects and system-level interactions. This makes it difficult to develop universally applicable, fundamental constitutive models for cyclic loading. Element-level cyclic simple shear (CSS) tests provide the ideal controlled dataset to isolate pure soil stress-strain response, but their integration into advanced predictive frameworks remains limited.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network (DeepONet) trained directly on a comprehensive database of element-level CSS tests can learn a generalized, accurate, and efficient constitutive operator for cyclic soil behavior, surpassing the predictive capability of traditional empirical models and purely data-driven neural networks.
proposals.method: We will compile a large, curated database of CSS test results from literature and new laboratory experiments, encompassing various soils, densities, and loading patterns. This database will train a Multiple-Input DeepONet, where the branch net encodes the loading path and the trunk net encodes soil state variables. Novel physics-informed loss functions will enforce fundamental constraints like hysteretic energy dissipation, the Masing rules, and critical state soil mechanics principles, moving beyond simple stiffness-based losses. Bayesian Optimization will be used to efficiently tune the network architecture and hyperparameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-constrained data-driven constitutive model directly grounded in fundamental soil element behavior. It will provide a more reliable and generalizable building block for larger-scale seismic site response and soil-structure interaction simulations. The framework will also serve as an explainable tool for interpreting the learned soil behavior from the trained operator.
proposals.novelty: While Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) have been applied to soil mechanics, the use of a Physics-Informed DeepONet to learn a full constitutive *operator* from element tests is novel. This approach explicitly decouples the loading function from soil state, enabling prediction for arbitrary cyclic loading paths not present in the training data, a significant advance over existing models.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Deep Operator Networks for Inverse Estimation of In-Situ Soil Constitutive Parameters from Slope Response
proposals.motivation: Determining accurate in-situ soil constitutive parameters (e.g., shear modulus degradation, damping) is critical for geotechnical design and hazard assessment, but direct measurement is often costly and invasive. The inverse analysis of slope response (e.g., displacement under load) offers a promising alternative, yet traditional methods are computationally expensive and suffer from non-uniqueness. This research addresses the need for a robust, efficient, and explainable framework to back-calculate soil properties from observable system performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A physics-informed Deep Operator Network (DeepONet) architecture, constrained by governing soil mechanics principles and enhanced with a stiffness-based loss function, can accurately and uniquely infer key soil constitutive parameters from measured slope displacement time histories.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel inverse modeling framework building on the PI-DeepONet architecture from the researcher's prior work. The network will be trained as a forward model mapping soil property functions to slope response. For the inverse task, a Bayesian optimization loop will be integrated to efficiently search the parameter space, minimizing the discrepancy between observed and predicted responses. The loss function will incorporate physics-based constraints (e.g., equilibrium, constitutive relations) and a stiffness regularization term to ensure physically plausible solutions and mitigate ill-posedness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a computationally efficient, physics-constrained tool for non-destructive site characterization, directly translating field monitoring data into engineering parameters. It will advance the field of geotechnical system identification by providing a novel integration of deep operator learning with inverse analysis, offering a pathway towards more reliable digital twins of soil-structure systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used physics-informed neural networks for forward response prediction, this proposal uniquely adapts the DeepONet architecture—a neural operator—for the geotechnical inverse problem. The integration of a dedicated stiffness-based loss and Bayesian optimization for explainable parameter search within this framework is a novel synthesis of the researcher's recent methodological developments.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed DeepONet Framework for Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction Analysis of Shallow Foundations on Slopes
proposals.motivation: The seismic performance of structures founded on or near slopes is poorly understood due to complex, coupled soil-structure interaction (SSI) mechanisms. Current design often relies on simplified, decoupled analyses, which can lead to unconservative estimates of foundation settlement, rotation, and structural damage during earthquakes. A robust, physics-constrained predictive model is urgently needed for risk assessment and resilient geotechnical design.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating domain-specific stiffness-based loss functions into a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture will enable accurate and efficient prediction of the dynamic response of a structure founded on a slope, capturing the non-linear, interdependent effects of slope geometry, soil properties, foundation characteristics, and seismic input.
proposals.method: We will develop a Multiple-Input DeepONet operator network where the branch net encodes variable slope geometry, soil profile, and foundation properties, and the trunk net handles the seismic time history. The core novelty is the integration of a physics-informed loss function that penalizes violations of force equilibrium and moment equilibrium at the foundation-soil interface, derived from simulated soil reaction profiles. Training data will be generated using high-fidelity 3D finite element models in OpenSees or PLAXIS, validated against centrifuge test data where available. Bayesian Optimisation will be used to tune the network's hyperparameters and loss weighting factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, explainable, and computationally efficient surrogate model for a critical SSI problem, directly applicable to performance-based earthquake engineering. It will provide new insights into the governing failure mechanisms for foundations on slopes and produce a validated tool for probabilistic seismic demand analysis. The integrated stiffness-based physics loss is a methodological advance for constraining neural networks in geotechnical applications.
proposals.novelty: While Physics-Informed DeepONets have been applied to fixed-base structures, their extension to slope SSI problems with a customized stiffness-based loss function governing the foundation interface is unprecedented. This moves beyond generic PDE residuals to embed mechanistic geotechnical principles directly into the learning algorithm.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Stochastic Deep Learning Framework for Time-Dependent Seismic Fragility Analysis of Geostructures
proposals.motivation: Current stochastic models for seismic performance assessment of geostructures (e.g., foundations, retaining walls) often treat soil and reinforcement properties as static, neglecting their long-term degradation due to environmental and cyclic loading effects. This simplification can lead to non-conservative estimates of structural reliability over a multi-decade lifespan, particularly for critical infrastructure. Integrating time-dependent property evolution is essential for accurate life-cycle risk analysis and resilient design.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating physics-based, time-dependent degradation models of soil stiffness and reinforcement corrosion into a stochastic, physics-informed machine learning framework will significantly improve the accuracy of long-term seismic fragility predictions for geotechnical systems compared to static-property models.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's established Physics-Infomed DeepONet architecture by integrating a stochastic time-evolution module. Soil shear modulus degradation and reinforcement bar diameter loss will be modeled as stochastic processes (e.g., using Gamma or Wiener processes) informed by established empirical/mechanistic laws (e.g., Idriss & Boulanger for cyclic degradation, fib Model Code for corrosion). These time-dependent parameters will serve as augmented inputs to a DeepONet, trained with a novel composite loss function combining dynamic structural response error, physics residuals (e.g., wave equation), and stiffness-based constraints that evolve with the simulated time parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational tool for time-variant seismic fragility analysis, directly linking material degradation mechanisms to system-level reliability. It will provide a more realistic framework for life-cycle cost and risk assessment of civil infrastructure. The proposed model will also serve as a benchmark for integrating stochastic temporal processes into physics-informed neural operators.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of stochastic time-dependent material degradation models with a physics-informed DeepONet architecture, creating a dynamic operator for seismic response that evolves with the asset's age, a significant advancement beyond static or purely data-driven time-series approaches in geotechnical earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: Full-Scale Validation of a Physics-Informed Graph Embedding Predictor for Resilient Sloping Foundation Performance Using Field Monitoring Data
proposals.motivation: The reliability of machine learning models for geotechnical and structural engineering hinges on validation with real-world data, which is often scarce. While advanced models like Physics-Informed DeepONets show promise for predicting dynamic responses, their application to complex systems like Resilient Sloping Foundations (RSFs) lacks rigorous field-scale verification. This gap limits the adoption of these data-driven tools for critical infrastructure risk assessment and design.
proposals.hypothesis: A Graph Embedding Predictor (GEP) framework, enhanced with physics-informed constraints and trained on a hybrid dataset, will demonstrate superior accuracy and generalizability in predicting the long-term performance and damage states of RSF systems when validated against full-scale field monitoring data, compared to purely data-driven or conventional numerical models.
proposals.method: We will leverage extensive field monitoring data (e.g., accelerations, displacements, pore pressures) from instrumented RSF projects. The core model will be a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based GEP, representing the soil-structure system as a graph of interacting components. Physics will be embedded via stiffness-based loss functions (as in PI-DeepONet) and by incorporating governing equations as soft constraints within the GEP architecture. A Bayesian optimization framework will be used for hyperparameter tuning and uncertainty quantification of the predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, field-scale benchmark for next-generation physics-informed machine learning in geotechnical engineering. It will deliver a validated, efficient predictive tool for RSF performance, directly supporting resilient infrastructure design. The study will also establish a framework for integrating heterogeneous field data into advanced hybrid models.
proposals.novelty: This is the first proposed full-scale validation of a graph-based, physics-informed predictor for a complex geotechnical system, moving beyond synthetic or centrifuge data. The integration of a GEP architecture with physics-based loss functions specifically for field data interpretation represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: A Real-Time Geotechnical Risk Dashboard for Rapid Seismic Foundation (RSF) Projects Using Physics-Informed DeepONet and Bayesian Updating
proposals.motivation: The design and construction of Rapid Seismic Foundations (RSF) in active seismic zones require immediate, data-informed decisions to manage risk. Current practice relies on periodic, offline analyses, creating a lag between data acquisition and actionable insight, which can compromise safety and efficiency. A real-time tool that integrates sensor data with advanced predictive models is critically needed to support engineers during dynamic construction phases.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a physics-informed Deep Operator Network (DeepONet) for structural response prediction with a Bayesian framework for real-time parameter updating will enable the creation of a dashboard that provides accurate, explainable, and continuously updated reliability assessments during RSF construction.
proposals.method: The core predictive engine will be a Physics-Informed DeepONet, pre-trained on high-fidelity simulations, to map real-time input streams (e.g., soil sensor data, construction progress) to dynamic structural responses. This will be coupled with an integrated Bayesian optimization and explainable AI (XAI) layer, similar to the researcher's prior work, to update soil/structure parameters and quantify prediction uncertainty. The dashboard will be developed as a web-based application using a Python backend (e.g., Dash or Streamlit) to visualize time-evolving risk metrics, model predictions, and XAI-driven insights.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully functional, prototype engineering dashboard that translates cutting-edge physics-informed machine learning research into a practical decision-support tool. It will provide a novel framework for continuous reliability assessment in geotechnical engineering, directly enhancing the safety and adaptability of seismic foundation construction. The integrated system will also serve as a testbed for evaluating human-in-the-loop interaction with AI-driven engineering predictions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time operationalization of a physics-informed DeepONet within a Bayesian updating loop specifically for a dynamic construction environment, moving beyond offline analysis to create a closed-loop, interactive risk management tool. This integration of real-time data assimilation, efficient operator networks, and explainable uncertainty quantification for dashboard deployment is unexplored in civil engineering applications.

proposals.title: Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Probabilistic Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification of Rapid Soil Foundation Settlement
proposals.motivation: Predicting rapid soil foundation (RSF) settlement is critical for the safety and serviceability of civil infrastructure, yet it is fraught with uncertainty due to complex, heterogeneous soil behavior. Traditional deterministic models or standard deep learning models fail to quantify the epistemic uncertainty arising from limited and noisy field data, which is a major limitation for risk-informed geotechnical design. A probabilistic framework that provides both predictions and a measure of confidence is urgently needed for robust decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian Neural Network (BNN), constrained by known physics, can provide accurate, probabilistic predictions of RSF settlement while explicitly quantifying the epistemic uncertainty inherent in the geotechnical parameters and model form.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Network (B-PINN) by placing probability distributions over the weights of a deep neural network architecture. The likelihood will incorporate a physics-based loss term, informed by simplified consolidation or settlement mechanics, alongside a data-fitting term. We will employ variational inference (e.g., Bayes by Backprop) or Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods (e.g., Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) for tractable posterior approximation, enabling us to generate predictive distributions for settlement from which mean predictions and credible intervals can be extracted.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, explainable probabilistic tool for geotechnical settlement analysis, directly quantifying prediction confidence. It will advance the integration of machine learning with geotechnical engineering principles, moving beyond point estimates to a full uncertainty quantification framework. The resulting model will provide engineers with crucial risk metrics to support resilient infrastructure design.
proposals.novelty: While PINNs and BNNs have been explored separately, their integration for geotechnical settlement prediction, specifically targeting the decomposition of epistemic uncertainty within a physics-constrained framework, represents a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond the researcher's previous explainable ML and DeepONet work by embedding Bayesian principles directly into the physics-informed learning process for uncertainty-aware outputs.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Site-Specific Soil Response Prediction with Sparse Data
proposals.motivation: Predicting soil response under dynamic loading is critical for seismic hazard assessment and geotechnical design, yet it is often hampered by site-specific data scarcity. Traditional empirical correlations lack generalizability, while purely data-driven machine learning models require extensive datasets and can produce physically inconsistent results. A method that leverages fundamental physics to guide learning from limited observations is urgently needed for reliable and efficient site characterization.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating governing soil mechanics equations (e.g., the wave equation, hyperbolic constitutive models) directly into the loss function of a neural network will constrain the solution space, enabling accurate and physically plausible predictions of soil dynamic response even with sparse training data from a specific site.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) where the primary network approximates the soil displacement field. The loss function will combine a standard data misfit term with a physics-based residual term, enforcing the 1D wave equation for vertically propagating shear waves and a simplified, differentiable soil constitutive model (e.g., a hyperbolic backbone curve). We will specifically adapt the Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, used by the researcher for structures, to handle soil boundary value problems, treating soil profile properties as functional inputs. Bayesian optimization will be employed to tune the weighting hyperparameters between the data and physics loss components.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool for geotechnical site response analysis that reduces reliance on large, costly datasets. It will advance the field of physics-informed machine learning by demonstrating its effective transfer from structural to geomechanics domains. The resulting framework will offer engineers a more robust, interpretable, and data-efficient method for predicting site amplification.
proposals.novelty: While PINNs have been applied in fluid dynamics and structural engineering, their tailored application to soil mechanics, integrating domain-specific nonlinear constitutive models into the loss for site response, is novel. This moves beyond generic PDE enforcement to encapsulate the essential hysteretic and pressure-dependent behavior of soils within a machine learning framework.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Stochastic Deep Learning Framework for Probabilistic Micro-Scale Soil-Reinforcement Interface Shear Modeling
proposals.motivation: The macro-scale performance of reinforced soil structures is governed by complex, stochastic interactions at the micro-scale of individual soil particles and reinforcement elements. Current design relies on empirical or deterministic models that cannot quantify the inherent uncertainty in interface shear behavior, limiting reliability assessments for critical infrastructure. A micro-scale probabilistic framework is needed to bridge the gap between fundamental mechanisms and system-level performance predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: The integration of physics-informed neural operators with a Bayesian stochastic framework can accurately model the probabilistic shear stress-displacement response at the soil-reinforcement interface, capturing the aleatory uncertainty from soil heterogeneity and epistemic uncertainty from limited micro-scale data.
proposals.method: We will develop a Physics-Informed DeepONet architecture, where the branch net encodes stochastic micro-scale parameters (e.g., particle morphology, local density, surface roughness) and the trunk net evaluates the shear displacement field. Physics-based constraints, derived from shear strength theory and energy balance, will be embedded via custom stiffness-based loss functions. A Bayesian optimization and explainable AI (XAI) pipeline, similar to the researcher's prior work, will be used for hyperparameter tuning and to identify the most influential micro-scale features governing interface reliability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational tool for probabilistic analysis of reinforcement-soil interaction, directly linking micro-scale variability to interface reliability. It will advance the field of geotechnical reliability by providing a physics-constrained, data-efficient model that can inform risk-based design and upscaling strategies for reinforced soil systems.
proposals.novelty: While Physics-Informed DeepONets have been applied to structural dynamics, their adaptation to model stochastic micro-scale geotechnical interface problems is novel. The proposed integration of a stiffness-based physics loss with a full Bayesian-explainable ML framework for interface shear represents a significant departure from traditional deterministic or purely data-driven micro-mechanical models.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed DeepONet Framework for Generalized Liquefaction Prediction in Complex Soil-Structure Systems
proposals.motivation: The LEAP (Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects) exercises have provided valuable benchmarks for validating liquefaction models, but they have primarily focused on simple, level, uniform soil deposits. Real-world geotechnical systems, such as layered soils, sloping ground, and embankments, introduce significant complexities that challenge the generality of existing predictive models. Extending LEAP-style validation to these complex geometries is crucial for developing reliable tools for assessing infrastructure resilience against seismic hazards.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a physics-informed machine learning operator, trained on a synthetically expanded dataset of complex soil profiles, will significantly outperform conventional and purely data-driven models in predicting the dynamic response and liquefaction potential of layered, sloped, and embankment systems.
proposals.method: We will generate a high-fidelity synthetic dataset using validated numerical models (e.g., OpenSees, FLAC) to simulate the seismic response of layered soils, gentle to moderate slopes, and typical embankment geometries. This dataset will be used to train and test a novel Physics-Informed DeepONet (PI-DeepONet) architecture. The network's loss function will be augmented with physics-based constraints derived from simplified governing equations (e.g., stress equilibrium, pore pressure generation models) and stiffness-based regularization terms, as demonstrated in the researcher's prior work, to ensure physically plausible predictions even for unseen profile configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new, open-source benchmark dataset for complex soil profile liquefaction, extending the legacy of LEAP; 2) a robust, physics-constrained predictive model that generalizes beyond simple uniform deposits; and 3) a framework for integrating domain knowledge into machine learning for geotechnical earthquake engineering, improving trust and interpretability.
proposals.novelty: While PI-DeepONets have been applied to structural dynamics, their adaptation to the coupled hydro-mechanical problem of liquefaction in complex, multi-layered soil geometries with free-field and sloping boundary conditions represents a significant and novel advancement beyond current state-of-the-art liquefaction prediction tools.

proposals.title: Generalizability Assessment of Physics-Informed Neural Operators for Seismic Response Prediction Under Diverse Ground Motion Characteristics
proposals.motivation: Current physics-informed machine learning (PIML) models for structural dynamics, such as DeepONets, are often validated on limited or synthetic seismic records. In real-world applications, earthquake motions vary significantly in frequency content and duration, which critically influences structural damage. A systematic evaluation of these models' robustness across a realistic spectrum of shaking types is lacking, hindering confidence in their deployment for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the predictive accuracy and extrapolation capability of physics-informed neural operators (e.g., DeepONet, MIONet) for structural response are sensitive to the frequency content and duration of input ground motions, and that incorporating these characteristics explicitly into the model architecture or training loss will significantly improve generalizability.
proposals.method: We will generate a comprehensive synthetic dataset using validated ground motion models and recorded motions from PEER NGA-West2, systematically varying key intensity measures (e.g., significant duration, mean period). A baseline Physics-Informed DeepONet, as developed in the researcher's prior work, will serve as the benchmark. Its performance will be evaluated across motion types, and failure modes will be analyzed. Subsequently, we will enhance the model by integrating a frequency-aware loss function, penalizing errors in modal response, and/or employing a multiple-input operator (MIONet) architecture that accepts both the acceleration time-history and scalar motion characteristics as separate, structured inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for benchmarking PIML models in seismic applications, identifying their limitations related to input motion diversity. It will deliver an improved, more generalizable neural operator model for structural response prediction, contributing to the reliable use of AI in seismic risk assessment. The findings will guide future development of trustworthy hybrid simulation and digital twin tools for civil infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While PIML for structures is an active area, a targeted investigation into the sensitivity of these models to fundamental ground motion parameters—and an architectural solution to mitigate it—is novel. This moves beyond single-record validation towards ensuring model reliability for the full range of expected seismic hazards.

proposals.title: Bayesian Calibration of a Physics-Informed Neural Operator for Sand Constitutive Modeling Using Direct Simple Shear Tests
proposals.motivation: Accurate constitutive model parameters are critical for reliable geotechnical seismic analysis, yet traditional calibration from element tests is often manual and subjective. This leads to significant uncertainty in predicting soil-structure interaction and site amplification. A rigorous, data-driven calibration framework is needed to reduce this epistemic uncertainty and improve the fidelity of numerical simulations for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian optimization framework, integrated with a physics-informed neural operator, can robustly and efficiently calibrate a sand constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) using a comprehensive dataset of Direct Simple Shear (DSS) tests, yielding a parameter set with quantified uncertainty that outperforms manually calibrated parameters in blind prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic series of monotonic and cyclic DSS tests on a uniform, clean sand (e.g., Ottawa F-65) to generate a high-quality stress-strain dataset. This data will train a Physics-Informed DeepONet, where the branch net encodes test conditions and the trunk net encodes strain paths, with loss functions enforcing elastoplastic consistency. A Bayesian optimization (BO) loop, using an expected improvement acquisition function, will then iteratively query the trained DeepONet surrogate to find the constitutive model parameters that minimize the discrepancy between simulated and experimental DSS responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, automated calibration pipeline that fuses high-fidelity element testing with advanced machine learning, providing a benchmark parameter set for the test sand with associated confidence intervals. It will advance the field by demonstrating how physics-informed neural operators can serve as efficient surrogates for accelerating inverse problems in geomechanics, leading to more reliable soil models for dynamic analysis.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a physics-constrained DeepONet as a differentiable surrogate within a Bayesian optimization loop for constitutive model calibration is a novel synthesis of the researcher's prior work. This moves beyond purely data-driven or manual calibration by embedding physical laws directly into the parameter inference process.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network for Inverse Site Characterization: Inferring Subsurface Density and Pore Pressure from Surface Seismic Motion
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical site characterization relies heavily on invasive and costly subsurface investigations. A non-invasive method to infer critical soil state parameters, like density and pore pressure, from surface motion data would revolutionize seismic hazard assessment, liquefaction prediction, and infrastructure monitoring. This is particularly urgent for rapid post-event evaluation and for sites where direct instrumentation is impractical.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physics-informed machine learning operator, trained on synthetic data from validated soil models, can accurately and robustly solve the ill-posed inverse problem of mapping surface acceleration time histories to spatially varying profiles of soil density and pore water pressure.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Physics-Informed DeepONet (PI-DeepONet) architecture. The branch network will encode surface motion (acceleration time series), while the trunk network will encode spatial depth coordinates. The operator's output will be the subsurface soil state profile. The key innovation is a hybrid loss function combining data misfit with physics-based constraints: the wave equation governing seismic wave propagation and constitutive soil model equations (e.g., a simplified hyperbolic or Drucker-Prager model) to ensure physically plausible stiffness and strength relationships linked to the inferred density and pressure. Bayesian optimization will be used to tune the network's hyperparameters and the weighting of the multi-term loss function.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind computational tool for non-destructive, full-profile soil state estimation using only surface sensors. It will advance the field of inverse problems in geomechanics by demonstrating how physics-informed operator learning can overcome ill-posedness. The method will provide a pathway for real-time subsurface condition monitoring for civil infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While PI-DeepONets have been used for forward structural response prediction (as in Abdoun's prior work), their application to the geotechnical inverse problem—specifically to infer two coupled, spatially varying state parameters from surface motion—is novel. The integration of stiffness-based constitutive constraints directly into the loss function to regularize the inverse mapping is a significant advancement over purely data-driven or simplified linear inverse methods.

ideas.title: Predictive Validation Framework for Soil Constitutive Models Using Tiered Experimental Data and Blind Tests
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous framework to move beyond simple curve-fitting by testing a model's true predictive capability. A varied experimental dataset is strategically partitioned: one portion is used for initial model calibration, while a separate set of 'blind' tests, designed to probe model boundaries and novel conditions, is withheld for final validation. The framework incorporates quantitative metrics to evaluate predictive accuracy and uncertainty when extrapolating. This ensures validated models are robust and reliable for forecasting soil behavior in new, untested scenarios critical for engineering design.

ideas.title: A Federated Learning Framework for Geotechnical Sensing using MEMS-Enabled CPT and Automated Data Protocols
ideas.description: This research fuses MEMS load sensor integration into CPT cones with automated, standardized data processing protocols to create a federated learning network for geotechnical sensing. Miniature MEMS sensors provide unprecedented spatial resolution of tip and sleeve stresses, generating rich, localized datasets at each participating centrifuge facility. The automated software protocols are extended to not only compare data but to implement a federated learning framework, where local models are trained on facility-specific MEMS data and only model updates (not raw data) are shared for aggregation. This synergy enables the collaborative development of advanced, universally calibrated soil constitutive models from distributed, high-fidelity experiments while maintaining data sovereignty and addressing site-specific variability directly.

ideas.title: Probabilistic seismic assessment using conditional spectrum-matched ground motion suites
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond using a single recorded motion or a simplified ramped sine wave by employing a large, site-specific suite of recorded ground motions. Each motion in the suite is rigorously selected and scaled to be consistent with a target conditional spectrum, which represents the expected frequency content given the occurrence of a ground motion with a specific intensity at the site. The structural response is then analyzed probabilistically across the entire suite, yielding statistical performance metrics that are directly tied to a defined seismic hazard level, providing a far more robust and generalizable basis for performance assessment and design.

ideas.title: Ambient vibration-based operational modal analysis with Bayesian uncertainty quantification for slope stability monitoring
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses output-only system identification techniques from structural health monitoring to characterize a soil slope's dynamic behavior using only its ambient vibration response. A dense array of seismometers or geophones continuously records microtremors induced by environmental sources (wind, traffic, microseisms). Advanced Operational Modal Analysis algorithms, such as Stochastic Subspace Identification or Frequency Domain Decomposition, extract the slope's stable modal parameters (natural frequencies, damping, and mode shapes). These identified parameters are fed into a Bayesian updating framework to track their statistical evolution over time, providing a probabilistic early-warning indicator of incipient slope failure based on changes in the system's dynamic signature.

ideas.title: Developing a CPT-Based Unified Soil Classification and State Parameter Framework for Complex Soils
ideas.description: This research inverts the CPT's role from a secondary quality control check to the primary instrument for defining fundamental soil state parameters. It achieves this by rigorously testing a new CPT device in centrifuge models featuring a strategically selected suite of complex soils, including cohesive clays, layered profiles, and silty sands. The core synergy is using the controlled, instrumented centrifuge environment to deconvolve the CPT's response across these varied soils, isolating the component attributable to intrinsic state. The outcome is a validated, soil-type-agnostic framework where CPT data directly yields primary state measurements, fundamentally changing how soil properties are characterized in practice.

ideas.title: Adapting Audio System Identification for Soil Slope Transfer Function Characterization
ideas.description: This idea adapts system identification techniques from audio engineering and acoustics to geotechnical engineering. In acoustics, the transfer function of a complex system like a concert hall is determined by injecting a known acoustic signal (e.g., a logarithmic sine sweep or MLS) and recording the output with microphones. Signal processing then extracts the system's impulse and frequency response. Adapted for soil slope analysis, a known seismic input (a controlled vibration with a broad frequency spectrum) would be applied to the slope crest or base using a vibroseis or impact source. The slope's response would be measured via a surface array of geophones. Advanced processing (e.g., cross-correlation, spectral division, or iterative deconvolution) would be used to compute the slope's transfer function, revealing its dynamic fingerprint—including key resonant modes, wave propagation paths, and energy dissipation characteristics—which correlates with internal structure, layer boundaries, and potential weakness planes. This provides a direct, empirical characterization of the slope's dynamic behavior for stability assessment.

ideas.title: Replace Synthetic Base Motion with a Library of Characteristic Earthquake Signatures
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a simplified ramped sine wave is a sufficient representation of earthquake base motion for structural analysis. Drawing an analogy from structural biology, where libraries of known protein folds replace purely synthetic simulations for predicting protein structure, the solution is to replace the synthetic wave with a curated library of characteristic earthquake signatures. This involves analyzing a vast database of recorded earthquake motions to identify recurring, fundamental patterns in ground acceleration (seismic motifs). These validated, representative signatures—capturing real-world complexity like near-fault pulses, long-duration shaking, or specific frequency content—would then form a standardized library. Structural testing and computational analysis would use these realistic, characteristic motions instead of the oversimplified ramped sine wave, leading to more accurate and reliable assessments of seismic performance.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Metric Portfolio Analysis from Finance to Geotechnical Response
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending correlation analysis to a broader suite of geotechnical response metrics by drawing an analogy from financial portfolio risk management. In finance, a portfolio's risk profile is understood through a correlated set of metrics (e.g., Sharpe ratio, VaR, drawdown), not just one. The analogous solution involves constructing and analyzing this multi-metric portfolio to capture interdependencies. Adapted to geotechnical engineering, the research would define a 'response portfolio' for a system, including metrics like seismic velocity, acoustic emissions, strain energy, and the rate of change of factor of safety. By exploring the correlations within this portfolio, the work aims to uncover predictive relationships and a more holistic view of system performance and pre-failure behavior, moving beyond traditional single or dual-metric analyses.

ideas.title: Inverse Calibration of CPT Systems via High-Fidelity Soil Replication
ideas.description: This research fuses the study of frictional and dynamic errors in the CPT system with the inverse modeling of soil preparation from CPT data. The core idea is to use the inverse soil creation procedure to physically replicate, in a controlled laboratory setting, the exact soil state inferred from field CPT data. By then performing high-g penetration tests on this replicated 'ground truth' soil using multiple CPT rigs, the systematic errors (sleeve friction, dynamic rod effects) inherent to each specific apparatus can be isolated and quantified. This enables a new paradigm of apparatus-specific, error-corrected interpretation of CPT data and the development of next-generation calibration frameworks that account for full system dynamics.

ideas.title: Validation and Calibration of Numerical Constitutive Models for Partially Saturated Soil Slopes
ideas.description: This research direction systematically employs a comprehensive experimental dataset, generated by varying saturation conditions from dry to partially saturated to fully submerged, to validate and calibrate advanced numerical soil constitutive models. The core novelty lies in using this varied data not just for point validation, but to develop and test unified models (e.g., extensions of the Barcelona Basic Model or similar frameworks) that can seamlessly transition between saturation states. This enables the predictive simulation of real-world slope failure scenarios triggered by rainfall infiltration, where the saturation condition is dynamically changing. The outcome is a robust, experimentally-grounded modeling capability for assessing slope stability under transient hydrological conditions, which isolated saturated or dry models cannot achieve.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Constraint of Soil Genesis Space using Adversarial Learning on CPT-Metadata Pairs
ideas.description: This research reframes the inverse problem by acknowledging its inherent non-uniqueness. A machine learning system is trained on a comprehensive, curated dataset linking detailed soil preparation metadata (e.g., gradation curves, compaction energy, moisture content, mineralogy) from multiple laboratories to their resulting CPT traces. Using an adversarial network architecture, the system learns to generate not a single procedure, but a manifold of plausible preparation protocols consistent with any given CPT signature. The final output is a probabilistically constrained 'genesis space' for the soil, providing engineers with a set of feasible, lab-replicable soil states that match field observations, along with explicit metrics of confidence and variability.

ideas.title: Intelligent Subset Selection for Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing in Structural Health Monitoring
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor arrays, adapted from aerospace applications, into structural elements like rods to provide continuous, distributed strain and temperature data. The key innovation is the development of an intelligent analysis framework that processes this dense sensor data stream to automatically detect and select specific spatial or temporal subsets of experiments or operational periods that exhibit anomalous behavior, complex loading patterns, or critical failure precursors. This enables researchers and monitoring systems to move beyond passive data collection to active, hypothesis-driven investigation, focusing computational and analytical resources on the most informative data segments. Consequently, the system achieves a synergistic capability for adaptive, context-aware structural health monitoring that prioritizes in-depth analysis of high-value events identified directly from the distributed sensor network.

ideas.title: Dual-Sensor System with Complementary Measurements via Geophysical Survey Analogy
ideas.description: This research expands the original dual-sensor seed idea by drawing an analogy from geophysical surveying, where combining complementary sensing modalities (e.g., seismic and GPR/ERT) through data fusion is standard practice to overcome individual method limitations. The analogous solution involves pairing deep-probing with near-surface or property-specific methods and fusing the data via joint inversion. Adapted to the context of soil characterization, the idea is to create a dual-sensor system that pairs the intrusive mechanical CPT probe with a non-intrusive, surface-deployed electromagnetic induction (EMI) sensor. The CPT provides direct, high-vertical-resolution data on stratigraphy and mechanical properties along its penetration path. The EMI sensor simultaneously collects data on bulk soil electrical conductivity, sensitive to composition (e.g., clay, water, salt content), over a larger horizontal area. The core innovation is the development of a data fusion and joint inversion framework where the precise CPT data acts as calibration points to constrain the interpretation of the EMI survey data. This fusion aims to generate a spatially comprehensive 3D model that integrates both mechanical strength parameters from the CPT and compositional properties from the EMI, significantly scaling the information obtained from a single sounding point to a continuous volume.

ideas.title: Inverse Soil Fabrication via CPT Data and Materials Genome Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'Materials Genome' inverse design paradigm from materials science to solve the geotechnical inverse problem of soil creation. In materials science, final material properties are linked back to their precise manufacturing history via extensive databases and algorithms. Here, we will compile a comprehensive database from multiple labs, pairing high-resolution CPT data from manufactured soil samples with the exact procedural 'recipe' used to create them (including particle size distribution, compaction method, water content, and placement technique). Using this database, we will develop and train an inverse modeling system. Given a target CPT trace as input, the system will identify the most probable preparation procedure or combination of procedures required to replicate that specific soil fabric and state in the lab, effectively providing a recipe for soil creation from its in-situ test signature.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Gold Standard' Paradigm: CPT as Primary Geotechnical Instrument
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from clinical diagnostics, where tools like PCR evolved from confirmatory tests to primary diagnostic standards, this idea challenges the foundational assumption in geotechnical engineering that laboratory tests on recovered samples are the primary source of truth for soil properties. The analogous solution was to re-evaluate the tool's fundamental outputs against clinical outcomes, develop standardized protocols for its direct use, and re-orient all other data to calibrate to it. Adapted here, the research idea is to invert the purpose of the Cone Penetration Test (CPT) from a quality control/verification tool to the primary density and soil property measurement instrument. This would involve developing a new theoretical and practical framework where the continuous, in-situ CPT profile is treated as the fundamental dataset, and all other measurements (e.g., laboratory tests on samples) are used to calibrate and interpret this primary signal, thereby minimizing sampling disturbance errors and creating a more direct link between in-situ measurement and engineering design models.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Data-Driven Centrifuge Testing with Real-Time Pore Pressure Feedback for Targeted Subset Analysis
ideas.description: This research integrates a robust 6mm piezocone with real-time pore pressure measurement into a centrifuge testing framework governed by an adaptive, data-driven control system. The system continuously monitors the high-fidelity pore pressure response during testing and uses predefined or machine-learned thresholds to automatically flag and isolate specific, critical experiment subsets—such as pre-failure pore pressure accumulation or post-liquefaction dissipation—for immediate, deeper analysis. This synergy moves beyond passive data collection and post-hoc selection, enabling experiments that dynamically focus investigative resources on the most geotechnically significant phases of soil behavior as they unfold under controlled centrifugal acceleration.

ideas.title: Using Multi-Metric Correlation Analysis to Diagnose Inter-Facility Centrifuge Discrepancies
ideas.description: This research proposes a systematic analysis where the correlations between a broad set of physical response metrics (e.g., accelerations, strains, bending moments, and pore pressures) are calculated and compared across multiple centrifuge facilities for nominally identical tests. The core hypothesis is that different sources of inter-facility discrepancy (e.g., boundary condition details, model preparation methods, or sensor calibration) will produce distinct, identifiable perturbations in the observed correlation matrices. By treating the correlation structure as a diagnostic signature, this approach moves beyond simply noting differences in single metrics to pinpointing the underlying mechanisms, thereby enabling targeted mitigation and more robust cross-facility validation of physical modeling.

ideas.title: High-Resolution CPT Calibration and Soil State Reconstruction Using Instrumented, FBG-Equipped Penetrometers
ideas.description: This research fuses aerospace-grade fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensor technology with inverse modeling from CPT data to create a next-generation instrumented penetrometer. The FBG sensors, embedded along the rod, provide continuous, distributed measurements of strain and temperature during penetration, offering a detailed, high-resolution 'fingerprint' of the soil response. This rich, direct sensor data is then used as the primary input for a sophisticated inverse analysis framework, moving beyond traditional tip and sleeve resistance to calibrate and validate the soil constitutive models used to replicate preparation procedures. The synergy enables the precise reconstruction of soil state and creation history, with the direct sensor feedback continuously refining the inverse model's accuracy for both laboratory and potential field applications.

ideas.title: Rapidly Fabricated CPT Cones for Active, Inverse Soil Fabrication Control
ideas.description: This research fuses rapid prototyping and inverse modeling to create a novel methodology for physical geotechnical model preparation. The core idea is to use 3D printing to fabricate disposable, instrumented CPT cones with tailored geometries and embedded micro-sensors for specific soil interrogation. These custom cones are then deployed in an iterative feedback loop: they perform CPT tests on a soil sample being prepared, the data is fed into an inverse model, and the model outputs adjustments to the soil preparation procedure. This cycle repeats with rapidly swapped cone designs until the in-situ CPT data matches a target dataset, thereby guaranteeing the replicated soil's mechanical properties are physically equivalent to the target.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Guided Correlation Framework for Multi-Metric Response Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a structured, hypothesis-driven framework to analyze correlations between forcing and diverse geotechnical or environmental response metrics. It begins by constructing explicit process-response models to define expected causal pathways, such as how seismic loading might influence gas emission rates or microbial community shifts. The analysis then employs statistical techniques designed to test these mechanistic hypotheses and control for confounding variables, moving beyond simple bivariate correlation. This approach ensures results are interpretable within a physical context, directly linking correlation patterns to underlying processes and increasing the actionable utility of the findings.

ideas.title: Applying Composite Material Failure Models to Heterogeneous Submerged Slopes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a clean, uniform sand in submerged slope experiments by introducing fines content or soil layering, drawing an analogy to composite materials science. In composites, introducing a second phase into a uniform matrix changes failure mechanics, solved by modeling interface strength and stress concentrations. Adapted to geotechnical research, the slope is treated as a 'geocomposite.' The study would experimentally and numerically characterize the interface shear between layers or mixed soils, specifically tracking how pore pressure dynamics and failure planes initiate and propagate through these deliberate heterogeneities, moving beyond homogeneous models.

ideas.title: Model-Agnostic Validation Framework for Centrifuge CPT with Epistemic Uncertainty Partitioning
ideas.description: This research establishes a validation protocol that separates apparatus performance from interpretive model choice. Multiple laboratories conduct CPT tests on replicated, benchmark soil specimens using a shared testing plan but their own scaling and interpretation models. The resulting dataset is analyzed using advanced uncertainty quantification to partition variance into aleatory (measurement, execution) and epistemic (model-form, scaling law) components. This creates a more robust benchmark that quantifies the true limits of knowledge in centrifuge CPT, guiding improvements in both experimental practice and constitutive modeling.

ideas.title: Use Gaussian processes for reliability-based design optimization
ideas.description: Employ Gaussian process regression as a surrogate model to enable efficient reliability-based design optimization of RSFs.

ideas.title: Adapting Medical Imaging Calibration for CPT Validation in Complex Soils
ideas.description: This idea adapts the medical imaging practice of using calibrated phantoms to validate new Cone Penetration Test (CPT) devices for complex soils. In radiology, new imaging devices are tested against physical phantoms with known internal heterogeneities to benchmark performance. Adapted to geotechnical engineering, the research would involve constructing standardized 'soil phantoms' for centrifuge testing—layered models with precisely engineered sequences of cohesive (e.g., clay) and granular soils, each with pre-characterized properties. The new CPT device would be deployed in these calibrated models to rigorously evaluate its accuracy in measuring strength and stiffness profiles and its sensitivity to detecting layer interfaces, providing a controlled, repeatable validation framework beyond uniform Ottawa sand.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Spatiotemporal Mapping of Suction and Strain in Partially Saturated Slopes Using Embedded Fiber Bragg Grating Arrays
ideas.description: This research embeds high-resolution Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor arrays, adapted from aerospace structural health monitoring, directly into soil nails or instrumented rods within a slope. By operating under controlled, partially saturated conditions, the system captures distributed strain from soil movement and precise temperature fluctuations caused by pore water evaporation or condensation. The synergy allows the coupled strain-temperature data to be used to back-calculate evolving soil suction profiles—a key stress state variable in unsaturated soil mechanics—along the entire sensor length. This creates a novel capability to visualize and model the transient, multi-dimensional wetting/drying fronts and their mechanical consequences in real time, moving beyond simple saturation states to active hydro-mechanical process monitoring.

ideas.title: A MEMS-Enabled Multi-Physics Cone Penetrometer for Real-Time Soil Behavior and Phase Identification
ideas.description: This research integrates an array of miniature MEMS load sensors directly into the cone tip and shaft with a co-located non-intrusive sensor, such as a micro-thermal needle or high-frequency dielectric probe. The MEMS sensors provide a spatially dense, dynamic map of local stresses and strains around the penetrating cone, while the complementary sensor simultaneously measures a bulk physical property like thermal conductivity or permittivity at the same location. This synchronized, multi-physics data stream allows for the direct correlation of mechanical response with soil composition and phase state, moving beyond empirical correlations to a fundamental, mechanism-based interpretation of cone penetration in complex soils like unsaturated sands, frozen ground, or gas hydrate-bearing sediments.

ideas.title: Full-Field Soil Density Inversion via Cone Penetration and Digital Image Correlation
ideas.description: This research fuses digital image correlation (DIC) for full-field soil deformation with the concept of using the cone penetration test (CPT) as a primary density measurement tool. The core innovation is to use DIC to capture the complete 3D soil displacement field around a penetrating CPT cone in a transparent calibration chamber. This full-field deformation data is then used to inversely calibrate and rigorously validate a constitutive model that directly relates CPT measurements (tip resistance, sleeve friction) to in-situ soil density. The result is a robust, physics-based CPT interpretation framework that provides a continuous spatial map of density, transforming the CPT from a discrete profiling tool into a primary instrument for quantifying full-field density heterogeneity and its evolution.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Data-Driven Calibration and Maintenance Intelligence System for Shared CPT Devices
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a static protocol by establishing a closed-loop intelligence framework for device stewardship. Each shared CPT device is equipped with a secure, tamper-evident logging module that continuously records operational parameters, calibration results, and environmental data. This stream of device-specific data is aggregated into a central analysis platform that uses statistical process control and machine learning models to identify anomalous performance, predict future calibration drift, and diagnose potential maintenance issues. The system then automatically generates and disseminates tailored calibration schedules and preventive maintenance instructions to receiving laboratories, creating a self-improving, evidence-based maintenance ecosystem.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Isolation Bearing Principles to Penetrometer Sleeve-Rod Dynamics
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a surface-mounted load cell eliminates all errors in penetrometer measurements by focusing on frictional and dynamic errors within the outer sleeve and rod during high-g penetration. Drawing an analogy from structural earthquake engineering, specifically seismic isolation bearing systems, the solution adapts the methodology used to model those decoupling interfaces. In seismic isolation, the frictional and hysteretic behavior of the bearings is meticulously characterized to understand force transmission and energy dissipation. Adapted to the penetrometer context, the research would develop a dynamic model of the sleeve-rod system, treating it as a coupled assembly with a frictional interface. The objective is to quantify the force losses and dynamic interactions that corrupt the load cell signal, enabling the development of correction factors or design modifications to account for these internal system errors.

ideas.title: Partial Saturation as a Phase Transition in Slope Stability
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that slope stability analysis can be adequately modeled by simply interpolating between end-member conditions of fully dry and fully saturated. Drawing an analogy from materials science and metallurgy, where phase diagrams describe abrupt property changes at specific compositional thresholds, the adapted idea proposes to treat varying saturation as inducing phase transitions in slope failure mechanisms. The solution involves experimental and modeling work to identify critical saturation degrees for different soil types where the dominant stability-controlling process shifts fundamentally (e.g., from cohesion and suction to effective stress and pore pressure). The outcome would be a 'slope stability phase diagram' that defines operational regimes and predicts nonlinear, threshold-based responses to partial saturation, moving beyond linear scaling of traditional safety factors.

ideas.title: Bio-inspired graded material layering for enhanced CPT structural resilience
ideas.description: This idea proposes to modify the mechanical design of CPT equipment to withstand more extreme centrifuge testing conditions (higher g-levels) or faster penetration rates by drawing an analogy from biomimetic materials science. The analogous problem of creating impact-resistant armor was solved by mimicking nacre's 'brick-and-mortar' microstructure, which uses a graded composite of hard and soft phases to deflect cracks and dissipate energy. The adapted research idea is to apply this principle of functionally graded, layered composites to the stress-critical components of the CPT probe (e.g., rod threads, cone body). By engineering a material structure with alternating zones of high stiffness and high toughness, stress concentrations from extreme dynamic loads can be managed more effectively, preventing brittle fracture and increasing the overall durability and performance limit of the tool.

ideas.title: Scale up centrifuge data to large earth dams
ideas.description: Apply GSL to predict performance of very large geostructures like tailings dams.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sampling for Targeted Experiment Analysis
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from geophysical surveying for mineral exploration, this idea proposes a two-stage, adaptive workflow for analyzing large-scale experimental data. Analogous to using broad surveys to find promising anomalies for targeted drilling, the system first provides researchers with a lightweight overview of all experiment runs. Researchers then interactively select specific, interesting subsets from this overview—such as experiments with unexpected outcomes, clusters of similar behavior, or boundary cases. Upon selection, the system automatically deploys a pre-configured suite of deeper, more computationally demanding analytical techniques (e.g., advanced statistical tests, parameter sensitivity studies, or detailed visual analytics) exclusively to that chosen subset. This adapts the resource-efficient 'survey-then-target' paradigm, allowing for focused, deep dives into the most scientifically valuable parts of the experimental data without the overhead of performing deep analysis on everything.

ideas.title: Robust, Self-Validating Bayesian Calibration with Integrated Discrepancy Modeling
ideas.description: This approach enhances standard Bayesian calibration with an integrated framework for continuous model criticism and adaptation. It rigorously tests the calibration model's assumptions using posterior predictive checks and structured cross-validation to detect facility-level misfit. A flexible, non-parametric module is trained to capture systematic residuals that the primary model misses, providing both a diagnostic and a corrective mechanism. The result is a self-validating system that delivers calibrated estimates while quantifying and mitigating the risk of its own model misspecification.

ideas.title: Bayesian Calibration Using Climate Model Ensemble Weighting
ideas.description: This research adapts the Bayesian Model Averaging (BMA) solution used in climate science to reconcile predictions from multiple global climate models (GCMs) to the problem of inter-facility variability in analytical or production methods. In climate science, BMA treats each GCM as a hypothesis, weights them based on historical performance against observations, and produces a probabilistically calibrated ensemble forecast. Similarly, we propose to treat each facility as a distinct 'model' implementing the core method. Using Bayesian calibration with shared reference data or standards, we will assign probabilistic weights to each facility's outputs based on their historical accuracy and precision. This adapted framework will generate a consensus prediction that accounts for facility-specific biases and uncertainties, providing a principled, probabilistic method to transfer and harmonize results across different sites without eliminating the informative variability between them.
